POETRY: At the Fall of the Curtain.. L. M. H. 9.
	The River of Life.......Lydia D. Alder 11.


AT THE FALL OF THE CURTAIN.

The curtain’s falling and the lights bum low,
So with God’s help I'm ready now to go;
I’ve seen life’s melodrama, paid the price,
Have known its loves and losses, hopes and fears,
The laughter and the tears.

And now, God knows, I would not see it twice !
I've crossed life’s ocean, faced its blinding foam,
And with four anchors cast, I’m nearing home.
And tho’ a storm-tossed hull I reach the shore,
A thing of tattered sheets and broken spars,
Naked against the stars,
I soon shall be at peace forevermore.

For if again I pass these waters through,
I know the kingdom I am sailing to,
What matters where the body lies—’neath sod,
Or down the dark impenetrable deep
Where the way-worn seaman sleeps?
All gates are good thro’ which we pass to God.
The night is dark, but God is in command;
And when the morning breaks I shall be “at the
	land."

Four anchors I cast out, Patience, Faith, Hope,
	Love !
O wisdom infinite! O Light-divine! 
How can I feel one fluttering doubt with anchors
	such as these cast out?
In His dear hand they’ll stay, I’ll wish and wait
	for day. 
	They cast out four anchors from the stern, and
waited for the day.—Acts 25th, 27th. 
			L. M. H.


[Column 1]
AUNT ESTHER’S SWEETHEART.
BY AUNT EM.

	“Auntie.” said a beautiful young girl,
in an absent-minded sort of way, turning 
half around on the piano stool, and with-
out raising her eyes from the floor, “I’m
tired, of this sort of thing, I want a change;
can’t we go somewhere or give a party;
can’t you suggest some amusement to pass 
away the time? Whv, I’ve not even had 
a letter for days, and that stupid brother of 
mine is having a jolly time of it at Har-
vard, while I’m here at home, day after day 
going through the same dull routine. Oh,
I’m weary, I’d even like to get married, if 
I could have an offer that suited me!”
	“Is that all, Alice? Have you no other 
complaints to make?” said her self-possessed 
aunt in reply. The girl looked a little 
crest-fallen, and without a word turned 
around and went on with her practicing. 
Her aunt, who was a wise and thoughtful 
woman, went on with her sewing as if 
nothing had been said; a long silence fol-
lowed, though the truth was Aunt Esther
didn’t know exactly what answer to make;
it was altogether new for her niece to burst 
forth so excitedly and it took the quaint 
little lady by surprise.

[Column 2]
	Alice sat playing a while longer, and
then came forward toward the fire, which 
was burning brightly, and seating herself 
comfortably in a lower rocker in the corner, 
began to stir the coals with the poker; a 
custom many people have when perplexed; 
the equanimity of her aunt was too much
for her; she wanted her to speak, say she 
had been rude, then she would have apolo-
gized; but to see her sit there so complacent-
ly stitching away, taking no notice of her 
vexation, was unaccountable to one so im-
pulsive. Finally she burst out again. 
	“Auntie, we are always going on in this 
humdrum way, for all the world like two 
old maids. You are not old if you are a 
widow, and I—well I am just twenty-two,
and have never yet had a real beau; I want 
some attention, some companionship.”
	“My dear,” said her aunt, “how long is 
it since we came home from the sea shore,
where you danced, and waltzed, and sang 
and took in all the gayeties of the season, 
until you were tired and wanted to come 
home to the Wren’s Nest, isn’t that so?”
	“Yes, Auntie, I did want to come home, 
thanks to your judicious training, because 
there was so much deception and hypo-
crisy there, and such ‘shilly shally’ girls,
and unprincipled young men, and old ones 
too for that matter, and mercenary, manag-
ing mammas, and all that; it was simply 
disgusting to sensible people.”
	“And that was society, Alice, the best
society, we are informed; and certainly if
wealth and extravagance are the standards
by which the ‘best society’ is estimated, it
must have been.”
	“Yes, Auntie, they were rich, I suppose, 
and they were proud and arrogant, but 
they were also ill-bred, some of them at 
least. Oh, I learned some lessons in the 
ways of the world that I shall never forget; 
and I did not hesitate to tell one young 
lady, who carried on a flirtation under my
very eyes, what my brother would have 
thought of her conduct, and she begged me 
to keep the matter secret. But a truce to 
this, we’ve talked of these things before,
I am positively pining for some one to talk 
to; I know you’re good company, and al-
ways amiable, but there’s no variety in our 
way of living. We’ve got lots of relatives,
let’s go and visit some of them, while
Harry’s at college; when he comes home 
everything will be different, he can take us 
out, and we shall have more society; it
won’t be near so lonely here then, he’s al-
ways so lively.”
	“Alice, what about the country cousins, 
do you remember where we used to visit 
when you and they were children?”
	“Yes, I recollect some of those quaint old 
places up among the New England hills,
they’re very nice in summer time, but 
what about winter, how would it be then?”
	“There’s skating and sleigh-riding, and
winter pastimes, of course; but you must 
choose, it is for your pleasure we shall go.”
	“Aunt Esther, are there any young peo-
ple at these places you think of—would it
be interesting in that way?”
	“In what way, Alice?”
	“Why, would there be any love

[Column 3]
making, or any chance of getting a good
husband?”
	“Alice, you astonish me; but just 
now you were deprecating the ways of 
those girls at the sea-shore, and now you 
are talking in much the same strain.”
	“But I am in earnest, I am going to mar-
ry some great, big-hearted, honest young 
fellow, that has never been in society; 
such as I had a glimpse of last season; and 
that reminds me, Auntie, that you have 
had a kind of romance in your life, I’ve
heard Polly say so; and you never say a 
word about it, nor your married life either, 
though for that matter I never asked you,
never thought of it in fact until now; will 
you tell me the story? It must be an inter-
esting chapter out of your history.”
	Before Aunt Esther had time to reply to 
this rapid questioning, a knock was heard 
at the parlor door and in came Polly with a 
handful of letters, and in the excitement
Alice forgot what she had said to dear
little Auntie, as she called her, and began
examining the postmarks, a habit with wom-
en it is said. “Here’s such a queer 
stamp; I do wonder where this letter’s
from? Some rich old uncle I hope, asking us 
to come for the holidays,” exclaimed Alice.
Then she took up the one she knew to be 
from her brother, and hurriedly broke the 
seal. While she sat reading it and tapping 
her dainty little feet on the carpet every 
now and then, a way she had when over-
joyed, Aunt Esther was busy perusing a 
letter that looked even more foreign than 
the one Alice had laid down. 
	In the midst of the reading a carriage 
drove up and visitors were announced, Mr. 
and Mrs. Elmore with their two daughters, 
and the letters were put aside; and the two 
ladies busied themselves entertaining their 
guests during the remainder of the after-
noon. The Elmores were neighbors of 
theirs, and very nice people indeed. The 
girls were near Alice’s age, and were both 
bright and clever. The afternoon sped 
swiftly away, and Alice had actually for-
gotten the letters, so absorbed was she with 
the conversation of their visitors. 
	Aunt Esther urged their guests to stay 
and dine with them, waiving ceremony, 
and they finally consented, so that it was 
nine o’clock or thereabouts before they 
were alone. The evening had been a 
pleasant one, and Alice was in the best of 
spirits, having thoroughly enjoyed the 
society of these neighbors, who had so much 
to say that was worth listening to. One of 
the young ladies was a fine singer and was 
much admired by Harry, who was passion-
ately fond of music, as indeed were both
Aunt Esther and Alice. Miss Elmore had
been singing, during the evening, one of 
Aunt Esther’s old favorites that had carried 
her back in memory to her girlhood days, 
and these words of the song kept ringing in 
her ears:
“Oh, no, we never mention him, his name is never
	heard,
My lips are now forbid to speak that once familiar
	word.”
	Aunt Esther, so calm and self-contented to 
all appearance, would have given almost any-


[Column 1]
thing to have been alone for a few minutes,
that she might weep to relieve her pent-up
feelings; but even when the guests had de-
parted Alice was there, and she must not
give way. The reading of the letters was
resumed, and for a while silence reigned.
What a pretty picture the two ladies made
in that elegant room! Alice was tall and
slender in figure, a perfect blond in com-
plexion, and this evening she wore a dress
of soft, pale blue merino, trimmed with
dainty lace, and as she sat reading, one
might have gazed in admiration, “and
turned and come again to take his fill” of
her rare beauty; every now and then
she spoke to Auntie and her countenance
would lighten to a glow, as she repeated
some of Harry’s witty and shrewd com-
ments upon his college life. Aunt Esther
was one of those peculiar types of women,
so difficult to describe that we term them
“a perfect study;” small in figure, dark
almost olive complexion, purplish or blue
black hair, large gray eyes; many people
considered her plain, but she was attractive,
possessed of that indescribable charm which
for want of a better term we call magnetic.
This evening she wore a gray dress of
some soft material trimmed with scarlet
velvet, and bending over her letters she
looked as much a picture as the young girl
who sat opposite; and Alice, chancing to
notice it, astonished her by saying, "Aunt
Esther, you look so charming tonight,
your very soul seems to be looking forth
out of your eyes. I wish you had a sweet-
heart, it’s such a lonely life to live; how long
since Uncle Herbert died?”
	"Ten years and over,” replied the little
woman, without once lifting her eyes.
Somehow her voice sounded strangely to
Alice, and as she looked she saw tears fall
upon the letter her aunt had been reading,
but which seemed to have dropped from
her hand. Alice knew her aunt was un-
demonstrative, and would not inquire what
made her sad, but thought it was her ill-
timed allusion to her uncle’s death; little
she knew the chord she had touched, that
made such discord in the heart of this staid
and demure little woman.
	The letters were finished and both ladies
drew nearer to the fire and resumed con-
versation, as all do who love each other,
just before separating for the night.
	"Alice, my dear, was your unknown
letter from some rich uncle—an invitation
for the holidays?”
	"No, Aunt, it was from old Mr. Brown
whom we met at the seaside last summer,
and funniest of all (for you remember how
I snubbed him) contained an offer of mar-
riage, the first one I ever had; (audacious
fellow!) tells me he loves me, and that he’s
worth half a million, all in one paragraph.
I wouldn’t marry him if he were worth
three millions; he’s a scoundrel, been in
society thirty years or more and divorced
a wife. Marry old Brown! I’d rather be
an old maid and stay with you all my days,
Auntie; you’ll never marry again, I don’t
suppose, though you’re much more fasci-
nating than most young girls, and know a
great deal more. Is that the reason they
call widows dangerous, I wonder? I’ve
often heard the expression, and couldn’t
imagine why it was used, for you are the
only young widow I have ever really been
acquainted with, and I’m sure you are the
most inoffensive and reserved, and the
very best little Auntie in all the world.”
As she finished her remark she rose and

[Column 2]
threw her arms around Aunt Esther’s neck
and deluged her with kisses.
	When Polly came in to inquire what
orders her mistress had to give before re-
tiring, Mrs. Danforth replied, “Nothing
tonight, but we are going away in a day or
two, so all our plans will be changed, and
we can dispense with some of the help for
the present; and Polly, would you like to
go anywhere on a visit?”
	“No, Mrs. Dantforth, I will stay and
take care of the house while you’re absent,
and Ben, of course you’ll retain him for
outside work; we two can do very well, and
it will save some expense.”
	“All right Polly, it’s very kind of you to
be so careful on my account; then I think
we shall pack up tomorrow, and go away
the next morning; good night, Polly.”
	“Alice,” said her aunt when they were
alone again, “I think I shall go to my sis-
ter’s in M—. Do you remember the
famous, rambling old country house with
the great, wide pastures? It was called
‘The Meadows.’ ”
	“You mean Aunt Jane’s. I remember it
a little, we used to play under the great
elm trees. Wasn’t there a lot of children?”
	“Seven, I believe, and they are none of
them married; you will not be lonely with
all these young people; at any rate we’ll
try it; I have not been there for many
years, never since my widowhood.” And
Aunt Esther paused for a moment, the
word seemed to stick in her throat. “It’s a
long way off, and we need not wait to send
them word, I am sure of a welcome, for
your Uncle William is one of the most hos-
pitable men I have ever known.”
	“Then, Auntie, that’s settled, and I
want you to tell me that romantic story of
your own life. Polly knows something
about it, so of course there is a story.”
	“What nonsense has Polly been filling
your foolish little head with, some servant’s
gossip I suppose?”
	“Oh no, Aunt Esther, this is quite differ-
ent, and if you don’t promise to tell me I
shall ask Polly to tell me before we go to
Aunt Jane’s, because one day she said to
me long ago: ‘Alice, you’re a lovely girl,
but not so attractive as your Aunt Esther
was; she has a history;’ and I wonder
Auntie that I never have teased you to tell
me the story.”
	“Do you see,” said her aunt, pointing to
the clock on the mantle, “it is after mid-
night, and we shall have a great deal to do
tomorrow; you really must go to bed now,
and forget all this foolishness about your
Auntie. It is quite enough for you to
know me as I am now and to judge me
upon my real merits, and not listen to
Polly’s insinuations; she’s over fond of me,
and her imagination carries her away
sometimes.”
	Alice was sleepy, and therefore bade her
aunt good night willingly; and Esther
Danforth sat there alone brooding over the
past that had been so vividly recalled to
her mind that day. For years there had
not been such a sealed fountain opened in
her soul as on that particular day. It
seemed as though that letter had brought
the past with it, not only to Esther herself,
but Polly and Alice seemed full of the
same reminiscences. She would not ac-
knowledge to herself that she had been
persuaded to marry for money, though the
world said so, the world in which she re-
volved; and tonight as she sat thinking, she
drew the letter from her pocket, the letter
that had excited Polly’s curiosity, and

[Column 3]
which seemed to have changed her plans
suddenly.
	“Shall I burn it?” she said to herself.
“I scarcely dare keep it. I am sure Polly
recognized the writing,” and as she solilo-
quized thus, she opened the letter, and was
again perusing its contents.  Looking over
her shoulder we might easily read it too,
for she held it wide open, and gazed as if
entranced. It ran thus:
My Precious Darling:
	I shall sail for America in a few days; I have
made definite arrangements; shall go by way of
London to transact some business there, and then
for Boston. In a few hours after arriving I shall
come to you, and learn my fate. I can no longer
endure this dreadful suspense; it is years since I
wrote you that first impassioned letter, after I
learned that you were free—free from that terrible
bondage of the heart. You answered me as
though you were an icicle, or that your heart
was frozen; but I know you better; you are mine;
and your own heart will bear you witness when
you read these burning words. You cannot forget
if you would the glorious past, the recollection of
which has hung over me during all these years of
banishment, like a rainbow of promise. I have
still the pledge of your devotion, given me on that
May morning when we plighted our troth under
the apple boughs, and I shall never part with it.
The last time I wrote—you answered me in doubt—
it gave mp some encouragement because you said,
“Wait, when the children I have charge of are not
dependent upon me for a home, I may consider
your proposal.” Must duty to the children of
your dead brother forever debar you from fulfill-
ing the pledge of your love to the living—to one
who has waited for you all these years—that he
might take you to his heart and shelter you there
forever? Esther, I am coming, and from your
own sweet lips I must hear the words that shall
decide my fate, my future life.
	Disguise your true self as you may to others,
you cannot deceive me; by my own strength of
affection I measure yours. I will not ask you to
answer me here, it would be too long to wait,
should you say yes; and so—farewell until we
shall meet, sweetest, dearest.
Your old sweetheart,
SIDNEY MANNING.
	Poor Aunt Esther, she read the letter
again and again, and her tears poured hot
and fast—she sobbed as if her heart would
break. “What shall I say to him when he
comes? How can I meet him again after
all these years? I dare not confess my
love, that has outlived everything. I will
not see him, I will go away, I shall not be
able to withstand his pleading!” Not-
withstanding her mental resolve, she folded
up the letter that had caused all this tumult
of feeling and put it in her bosom, and as she
passed out of the room and ascended the
staircase she moaned, and every now and
then she leaned her head upon the balu-
strade and would have fallen but for its
support. Little sleep for her that night;
while Alice, who was wholly heart-free,
slept the sweet sleep of youth and inno-
cence. How true the poet’s words:
“Tired nature’s sweet restorer, balmy sleep; he,
	like the world,
His ready visit pays, when fortune smiles; the
	wretched he forsakes;
Swift on his downy pinions, flies from grief,
And lights on lids unsullied with a tear.”
	The household were early astir the next
morning, for Polly, the faithful friend, had
been almost as wakeful as her mistress, and
the day was a busy one with preparations
for the journey and the necessary changes;
the trunks were packed and strapped and
traveling bags made ready, and Esther Dan-
forth, like one in a dream, went from room
to room to see that all was safe. “This
was his home,” she muttered under her
breath, “my husband’s;” and a cold shiver
ran through her veins; “and I was almost
persuaded to leave it once, but my good


[Column One]
angel stood by and saved me—and Polly,
dear, good girl, she helped me to bear it
all. I came here a bride from the church
door, with showers of congratulations;
what a wretched creature I was; with my
breaking heart and my broken vows; and
Sidney, I did not even dare to think what
would become of him. I was young then,
so very young to be an old man’s bride;
and he knew I did not love him, but he
was ever kind, too kind, and he left all
his wealth to me—only I must not marry
Sidney Manning—there it is in his will,
and it was read that day, when all the
family were there. What a reproach to me
and how they all stared, until I fainted.
I must not marry Sidney Manning ! No,
though my heart should break; not be-
cause of the money—what do I care for the
money; yet it has been a blessing to my
brother’s children, left orphaned and desti-
tute,” and Esther Danforth sank upon her
knees, there in the pale moonlight, be-
seeching heaven to guide her footsteps; and
her prayer was answered.
	The journey occupied two days and
nights; for those were not the times of
steam and rapid transit. At the close of
the second day they reached their destina-
tion. It was a bright, clear, moonlight
night, and the great wide-spreading elms
stretched out their branches, as if to wel-
come and shelter them	The greetings
were affectionate, and Alice was quite de
lighted at the reception she received; it was
so genuine and cordial. The travelers
were tired and glad to go rather early to
bed, especially as they were aware that, as
they were in the country, they would be
expected to rise early.
	Time passed quickly with Alice; there
was so much that was new and pleasant,
and the three young men, her cousins, vied
with each other in planning fresh sur-
prises for her amusement. Aunt Esther
was visiting in a quiet way with her sister,
from whom she had been so long separated,
there were so many things to tell each other
and it was nearing Christmas, and a grand
party was to be given in honor of the visit
of Mrs. Danforth and her niece. Aunt
Jane, as Mrs. Wetherell was called, was
half sister only to Aunt Esther and Alice’s
father, and was some years older she was a
very estimable woman and religious in her
nature; had studied the Bible and sought to
comprehend its meaning. Of late, a new
light had shone across her pathway, and
she had been so impressed with the divine
source from which it came, that she was
making a careful and prayerful examina-
tion of the teachings she had received from
the messengers who came to her with glad
tidings. Her husband was investigating
the doctrine too, and ere long they unfolded
the views they entertained to Aunt Esther.
Contrary to their expectations, she seemed
to grasp these new truths more quickly
than they had done, and it was a confirma-
tion to them that the doctrine was from
heaven. Aunt Esther had gone into the
country place with a broken heart, asking
help from One above, and so completely
humble was she, that she was ready to re-
ceive heavenly truths; in fact she scarcely
needed preaching to, her mind was so clear
on the subjects presented to her by her sis-
ter and her husband.
	The young men were somewhat favorably
impressed with the new doctrines, and had
talked to Alice about the everlasting Gos-
pel, but none of them as yet had been bap-
tized, they were waiting for some Elders,

[Column Two]
who were shortly to pay them a visit and
preach to the people in that vicinity.
	The “Mormon” Elders soon came to
M——, and Mr. Wetherell, who was a
man of considerable influence, tried to get a
hall or meeting house for them to preach in;
but he met with many obstacles in his
efforts that he had not previously antici-
pated; jealousy and tradition were rife in
the hearts of those who were opposed, and
the good man at last decided that the meet-
ing should be at his own place, “The
Meadows.” The house was large and
roomy, and he had expected to entertain as
his guests those who would come to preach
and baptize. All the people in the neigh-
borhood were cordially invited, and many
came to the meetings, and some few con-
verts were baptized and confirmed after two
days’ meetings had been held. Among
those who were baptized were Mr. and Mrs.
Wetherell, Mrs. Danforth and Alice May-
nard and the three sons and four daughters
of the host and hostess of “The Meadows.”
There were eight others baptized and many
more believing. Among the latter was a
young man whom Miss Alice greatly ad-
mired; he had already shown her some
marked attention, and her resolve to marry
some honest, big-hearted fellow was in a
fair way of fulfillment. He was a fine
figure, large and well proportioned, and his
education had not been neglected, even
though he was a farmer’s son, and lived
among the New England hills. He rather
astonished Miss Alice one day by telling
her he had never been in Boston; but she
recovered herself quickly and replied, “she
was glad of it.” Not but that she was
proud of Boston too, but somehow she
liked him better as he was; better than she
ever thought she could like any of the
young men she had met at the capital.
	Meantime both Aunt Esther and Alice
wrote to Harry of the religion they had em-
braced; they were full of zeal and desirous
that he should become a convert. But he
was at that time under quite a different in-
fluence, and his answers to their letters
were very unsatisfactory; but they did not
lose courage. Alice was using all her elo-
quence at home to persuade Richaid Ash-
burn to join the Church of Latter-day
Saints, and her efforts were not in vain; be-
fore many days she had the satisfaction of
knowing that he had espoused the same
religious faith.
		[To be continued.]


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


A WINDY DAY. 
	The fourth of May was the day appointed 
for a reunion at Idaho Falls, a reunion of 
the Relief Society workers in the Stake. 
	In spite of the dreadful storm that pre-
vailed the meeting house was comfortably 
filled. For my part, I would have given up 
the idea of driving the seven miles there, 
but for the fact that I had promised to call 
for two other sisters on the way; so for that 
reason, and as I had my picnic all prepared,
I thought I would venture, hoping that the 
storm would diminish before long. How-
ever, when I came within two miles of the 
Falls, the clouds of dust were so thick and 
dense that twice I could not see the horses'
heads, and could not tell whether they 
were keeping the road or not.
	I do not know when I was so surprised 
at anything as I was to see a few young 
mothers with babes in their arms at that 
meeting. True it is that when the Mor-
mon people make an appointment, they do


not allow anything to prevent them from 
fulfilling it. 
	Sister Bennett said she was sorry that 
two of the sisters whose names were on the 
program were not there, on account of a 
small fire that had occurred in that town in 
the morning. She also spoke of the dance 
that was to convene at eight o’clock in the 
evening, said she thought eight o’clock
was a good time to commence a dance, as 
she, with others, regretted that so many 
dances up here in Idaho did not commence 
until ten o’clock and after; for her part, all 
the enthusiasm for a dance was about gone 
at that hour. 
	When my daughter was up here last 
summer on a visit, she remarked how late 
the young people’s dances took up, and 
said they commenced earlier in Taylorsville.
I said I wished the young people would 
take that step, it would be a step in the 
right direction, for it was a great tax on 
their bodies, especially when they had to 
go to school or to work the next day.
Sometimes it is three or four o’clock be-
fore the dances break up, and perhaps some 
of them have an hour’s drive before they 
get home. If they were obliged to do it 
they would think they were misused. But 
most of them wait for the rest; they must 
talk this up among themselves, and then 
the wisest will take the lead and this late 
hour system will, I hope, be an event of 
the past. 
				LITTLE SISTER.


THE RIVER OF LIFE. 
Broad and majestic gleams its broad bosom,
As rippling it flows to the setting sun;
Ships sail upon it so jaunty and gay—
Their course on the journey has just begun. 
The glint of the sun above the horizon, 
Softly touching their white fluttering sails,
In glory soon bursts dispersing the shadows,
And never the shore wind stops or fails.

Laughter and song, floating out amid music,
Are borne o’er the water, so clear, so blue;
Other ships that are sailing can hear them,
Then join in their dancing and melody too.
From their decks are seen wrecks far away,
Of ships that were fair and oft braved the gale;
Now they are broken, rudderless, wand’ring,
How piteous and sad, how mournful the tale. 

Still onward are sailing some ships of the Past,
Nor song, nor music is heard on them more;
Some whirl with the tide where waters fast foam,
And sink out of sight not far from the shore.
The flap of the sails, the large broken bow,
That brilliantly shone and glistened at morn,
'Neath darkening waves sinks from mortal eyes,
A wreck, a wreck, bright hopes are dead and
	forlorn.

Some still sail stately, standing proud to the breeze,
And over the breakers come safe at last;
Riding into the harbor rejoicing,
For the storms of life overcome, are past.
In the shining waters of heaven’s smooth river,
Safely they anchor under clear, sunny skies,
The shore so fair in the dreamy distance,
Is the glorious land—the fair Paradise.
				LYDIA D. ALDER
May, 1899.

SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JUNE 15, 1899


[Column 1]
“THE FIRST OF JUNE,” 1899, AT SALTAIR.

	The day was not so fair and beautiful as
the celebrating relatives and friends of the
honored and beloved Prophet, President
Brigham Young, might have wished for
his ninety-eighth birthday. But notwith-
standing the fact that sudden gusts of wind
and intermittent rain showers prevented a
great many from venturing out to the Lake,
there were still many hundreds that did
venture, and were well paid for doing so.
And when it is taken into consideration
that Sister Zina D. H. Young was at Salt-
air, the place appointed for the great cele-
bration, from 10 a. m. until 7 p. m., and
received no harm but was feeling quite
well the next day, it is easy to believe that
no participant received anything but good
from the day’s outing.
	A description of how the tables were
spread and the edibles served and enjoyed,
or of the dancing and gaiety with which
the celebration closed at 10 in the evening,
can hardly be given space in this brief
notice. Those pleasant festivities, however,
will long be remembered with peculiar
satisfaction by many of the younger and
gayer portion of the committee. And the
program proper was excellent.
	At three o’clock when Elder B. Morris
Young announced that the program was
about to commence, a happy circumstance
occurred for rather a small part of the
listeners. It was that they found them-
selves in comfortable seats near enough to
the stand to be able to hear distinctly what
would be said from it. The only regret
then for such ones was that so many of
their friends were not equally well provided
for.
	Some of the members of the Temple choir
with their conductor, Brother Charles J.
Thomas, opened the exercises by singing
President Young’s favorite hymn, “O, my
Father,” in the old, familiar tune to which
it was first sung. This brought tender re-
membrances of childhood’s days, when
“little Mormons” learned to sing those
soul-inspiring words in their humble homes,
and to better understand their true relation-
ship to Deity because of the grand lesson
they so plainly teach. To one “little Mor-
mon,” at least, those lines were a first intro-
duction to the name of their gifted author,
Eliza R. Snow.
	After prayer by Elder Royal B. Young,
and singing again by the choir. Apostle 
Brigham Young made a short but feeling

[Column 2]
address, in which reference was gratefully
made to the appropriateness of the gather-
ing in honor of his father’s memory. And
he blest the people much as his father used
to do in such assemblies.
	Apostle F. M. Lyman told of the great
love the Prophet Brigham Young had for
his people, his wisdom in leading them,
and the consequent love and confidence
the people always felt and manifested to-
wards him. Choice selections by the band
were next rendered.
	Senator Joseph L. Rawlins claimed for
Brigham Young the right of recognition as
a great man, not only among his own peo-
ple, not only in his own nation, but in the
history of mankind; and said that ere long
his statue would be placed alongside of
those of other great men in the statuary
hall at Washington. The enduring super-
structure upon which this State—regarded
as one of the most substantial, intelligent
and progressive in the Union—has been
erected, the Senator attributed to the wise
and earnest teachings of Brigham Young to
his people.
	Governor Heber M. Wells, in tender,
loving memories of his “great, dear, kind
Uncle Brigham,” his “father’s best friend,”
spoke the sentiments of a large majority of
the people of Utah who were little children
in the early days of its settlement. The
tribute he paid to Brigham Young’s great-
ness and goodness, the grandeur of his
character, gave voice to the impressions
which are stamped indelibly upon the
hearts of thousands and tens of thousands
of those who were children thirty, forty and
fifty years ago. Of the greatness of Presi-
dent Young as a leader, a statesman and
builder, and his goodness as a kind and
wise father of his people, and friend of man-
kind, nothing seemed to be omitted in the
Governor’s remarks. It was clearly shown
that none but a large-souled, generous and
pure-hearted man, highly favored, inspired
and endowed with power from on high,
could have been what Brigham Young was,
or accomplished the work which he did.
	Judge Kinney, who was chief justice of
Utah Territory some years in the Fif-
ties, and came here recently from California,
was introduced to the assembly and re-
ceived with warm applause. For he was
remembered by many as “a friend to Brig-
ham Young and his people,” an unusual
 thing for a government official in those
early days of misrepresentation and perse-
cution.
	By the Judge was given a character
sketch of President Young, illustrated by
telling incidents with which he was himself
personally connected. He placed the great
leader in the true light of a mighty man,
wise, just, honest and benevolent.
	While Judge Kinney related historic cir-
cumstances of more than forty years ago, a
certain copy of the Deseret News presented
itself to the mind’s eye, recalling vividly to
memory the fact that the Judge had, dur-
ing those days of his residing in Utah,
suffered a great loss which might have
helped to strengthen the bond of sympathy
which was created between himself and the
man of whom he was speaking. Surely he
had known sorrow, and evidently the voice
of sympathy found utterance for him. The
copy of the News referred to contained
something in connection with the name of
the speaker which made so deep an im-
pression upon the mind of a child as to be
distinctly remembered while listening to
his words, although not seen, perhaps

[Column 3]
not thought of, for years and years. The
well remembered article was as follows:

LINES
Affectionately inscribed to Judge and Mrs. Kin-
ney on the death of their little son Renfrew.
BY S. E. CARMICHEL.
THE BEAUTIFUL ONE WITH GOD.
“The roses bloom, the roses fade;
The sunlight comes and goes;
The moonbeams weave a silvery braid,
With the white, drifting snows.
But the call of spring will never bring,
Nor the smile of the morning wake,
The floweret fair with the shining hair,
That the angels came to take.
And never again will a path of pain
By his shuddering feet be trod;
There are no tears in the coming years,
For the beautiful one with God.

“The roses bloom, the roses fade,
Fade over his early sleep;
O’er the soft brow under the grasses laid,
Stilled by a calm so deep!
But the look of care on his forehead fair.
The smile that his bright lips wore, 
And the soul replies of his sunny eyes,
These are of earth no more.
These were not laid ’mid the grasses’ shade,
Nor pillowed beneath the sod;
And they live unveiled where the stars are
	paled,
With the beautiful one with God.”

	To conclude the program, Dr. Seymour
B. Young sang another favorite song of
President Young’s, “Hard times come
again no more,” the congregation joining
in the chorus. With this came pleasant
memories of how the doctor used to sing
in former days; and how our mothers have
told us of still earlier times, when the 
brother Joseph and Brigham were called
“The sweet singers of Zion.”
	The benediction was pronounced by
Stake President Angus M. Cannon.
	Although socially the celebration was an
admirable success, on account of the threat-
ening aspect of the weather the attendance
was not sufficient to secure financial satis-
faction The returns, if there were any,
after expenses were all paid, were so small
that the committees design making another
effort in the near future, to raise means by
which to assist in paying off the still re-
maining indebtedness for the Brigham
Young Monument.
	The fact that Presidents Lorenzo Snow
and Joseph F. Smith and several of the
Twelve Apostles were present at Saltair for
a portion of the day, should not be over-
looked, although none except those already
mentioned took part in the program.
						L. L. G. R.


A WOMAN MISSIONARY.
[Mrs. Addison Pratt on the Society Islands 
1850.]
After exchanging tears and adieux six
wagons started on, twenty-four persons in
all, expecting to overtake a small company
at Bear river. We found the Weber river
high, the water running over the middle of
the bridge. We got safely over, called at
Brown’s Fort, where dinner was prepared
for us by a good sister whose name is
Abigail Abbot, a neighbor in Nauvoo. She
made me a present of a good cheese. Her
daughters accompanied us on horseback
several miles.
We came to Ogden river; had an almost
perpendicular hill to come down. The men
let the wagons down by the help of ropes;
had a severe time crossing the water. After
we crossed the bridge the wagons mired.
The men were in the water up to their
waists more than an hour. Mr. Tompkins
was wroth because some who got over first


[Column 1]
did not come back to help others. He had
cause to be angry. After we were out of
the water it began to rain severely. We
traveled three miles and camped at Brother
Chase's to wait for fair weather. We heard
afterwards the Weber bridge washed away
the night after we crossed over. Who can
say we did not cross in the right time?
	We came to Bear river; found President
Clark's company waiting for us. With
great exertion we got the cattle over; camped
on the bank.
	The 13th day we crossed Malad creek,
one of the most intolerable places my eyes
ever beheld. Cattle and men up to their
backs in mud for twenty rods and more.
What a world is this! thought I. Men
will travel over it, however impossible it
may seem to be. We got out of the mud
at last; camped at two o'clock. I have a
great aversion to camping before night. I
cannot feel well when a good day's drive
has not been accomplished.
	In the afternoon the company was organ-
ized, passing resolutions to keep the Sab-
bath day, likewise to have morning and
evening prayers. H. Clark was president;
W. Huntington, counselor. We have
music in the camp, two violins and a flute.
Besides there are many singing birds in this
country, which delighted me very much.
Now twenty four miles from Bear river, a
place of hills and dales. Sister C. Crosby
and I walked out among the hills. As we
were returning, two bullets whistled by our
heads; we started with fear! We came to
camp; found Hanks and Moses had been
firing guns for sport, not knowing any one
had gone out. They were admonished to
be more cautious. The evening was spent
in singing hymns and spiritual songs.
How comforting to have prayers in this
lonely spot of earth! What an idle life to
travel with ox teams! And yet no other
would do so well on such roads. I gaze
around me; see the wagons all corralled;
cattle lying down at night. Scenes of other
days come vividly to mind. What wander-
ing pilgrims we have been!
	The 18th day traveled twenty miles;
camped at three o'clock. I walked alone;
climbed upon a huge pile of rocks. From
their heights I had a full view of the sur-
rounding country. I called to mind the
lapse of ages those cragged rocks had re-
mained there! They were covered with
moss of various colors. I examined every
part of them with intense interest. I gazed
towards the valley, where I had left the
Saints; an ardent desire sprang up in my
heart that I might live to return to them.
	The 23rd, on account of the Cazyer river
being too high to be forded, we had to go
around the mountains. We came to camp
at four o'clock; called the place City Rocks.
We all went out on an exploring excursion;
returned fatigued, but much amused. The
beautiful white rocks resembling marble
made us wish they were in some location
where they could be made available.
	21st. We have been traveling over moun-
tains all day, the most delightful scenery
my eyes have ever beheld. Ridges of hills
of all sizes and shapes, variegated with
shrubbery, mostly cedar. The hills resem-
ble the orchards of New England. What a
prospect for a painter! I found stones I
called carnelian. White clay, of which the
boys made pipes, was found in quantities.
Snow storms overtook us; we could not
start till one o'clock. Mr. Tomkins found a
sheet-iron stove and gave it to me. I found
it very useful.

[Column 2]
	The 30th day, started very early, trav-
eled a short distance; came to a stream of
water called Martin's Fork. We found
written on a board, "Too deep to ford, re-
move your loading, prepare a wagon bed
for a boat." We did so and all got safely
over with hard labor. Very deep mire on
both sides. The brethren were all merry,
making a scene of amusement of it.
	The 31st, crossed one of the tributaries of
the St. Mary's river. Made a bridge of
willows, drew the wagons over by hand.
When the last wagon was over the men
commenced halloing at the top of their
voices. The next crossing was effected by
doubling teams.
	The 1st day of June crossed Martin's
Fork. The water being high, we took
everything from the bottom of our wagons
and piled them on our bedsteads; by that
means we got safely through. The same
day one of the Indian boys belonging to
the company put a can of powder into my
stove. Not for mischief; but coming in
from hunting, the stove being tied on the
outside, he thought to remove it as soon as
we came into camp, but, boy-like, he for-
got it. I built my fire and put my kettle
on. Once I removed the kettle, and lean-
ing over, stirred the fire. My cow was
grazing a little way off. An impression
came to me, as if some one had whispered,
"Walk out where your cow is and look at
her." I did so, and while I was standing
there I heard a report like a cannon fired.
I returned immediately, found my stove
blown over the top of a covered wagon. It
was crushed as with a sledge hammer. My
kettle was set upon the ground badly
bruised. The boy was severely repri-
manded, and I had serious reflections the
remainder of the day. The fact of my be-
ing warned to walk away from the spot an
instant before the explosion, caused me to
realize that a protecting care was held over
me, and that my guardian angel must have
been very near me, for which I felt thank-
ful. Some there were who felt as I did,
were serious over the affair. The more
giddy portion were disposed to ridicule the
effect it had upon me, and in their fool-
hardiness would laugh at the idea of being
solemn at the instance of so marvelous an
escape.
[To be continued.]


WGMAN'S EXPONENT. 13


[Column 1]

INTERNATIONAL COUNCIL.

DATES AND DISTRIBUTION OF MEETINGS.
The meetings will open on Monday,
June 26th, at 2:30 p. m., and will conclude
on Wednesday, July 5th, with a session con-
vened at 10 a. m.
The latest revised notes of the program
show that there will be five sessions of the 
International Council, as follows:
Monday, June 26th, 2:30 p. m., in the
Large Hall, Westminster Town Hall.
Wednesday, June 28th, 10 a. m., Coun-
cil Chamber Westminster Hall.
Thursday, June 29th, 10 a. m., Large
Hall, Westminster Town Hall.
Tuesday, July 4th, 10 a. m., Large Hall,
Westminster Town Hall.
Wednesday, July 5th, 10 a. m., place
not yet selected.
The International Congress, convened
under the auspices of the International
Council is divided into sections, as fol-
lows:
I. The Educational Section, which will
hold eleven meetings, most of which are

[Column 2]
set for the Large Hall in Westminster
Town Hall.
II. The Professional Section, which
will hold fifteen meetings in the Council
Chamber in Westminster Town Hall, and
in different apartments in St. Martin’s
Town Hall. 
IV. The Political Section, which will
hold five meetings in the Great Hall in St.
Martin’s Town Hall.
The Social Section, which will hold
eleven meetings, chiefly in the Convocation
Hall of Church House, Westminster.
The number of speakers, their representa-
tive positions, and: the different countries ot 
which they belong indicate a program of
unprecedented versatility and value.
Letters yet arrive by almost every mail
asking questions, which I shall seek to
answer in this article.
I. Who are eligible to attend meetings
of the Council and the Congress? The pub-
lic meetings of both, that is, the meetings
at which the programmes are rendered,
may be attended by anyone wishing to buy
tickets. The applicatio[-]s for these tickets
are in three forms:
(a) Is for ordinary tickets of member-
ship in the Congress, the price of which is
seven shillings and sixpence, or, in Ameri-
can currency, $1.75.
(b) May be applied for by any member
of any organization affiliated to any Nation-
al Council, the price of this is five shillings,
or in American currency, $1.25.
(c) May be applied for by any official
of any organization affiliated in any Nation-
al Council.
These forms may be had upon application
to the writer of this letter. The money
for tickets must in all cases be sent to the
Honorable Treasurer, from whom tickets
may be secured, International Congress
Fund, General Post Office, London.
These tickets, it will be understood, ad-
mit only to the public meetings. The
business meetings of the International
Council are only open to the following:
The officers of the International; the ac-
credited delegates from each National Con-
gress; the honorary president and delegates
from countries where no National Councils
are yet formed.
II. Who are the delegates to the Coun-
cil? Delegates may be sent to the Inter-
national Council only by National Councils
affiliated with the International Council.
Each National Council may send two dele-
gates, who, with its president, constitute
the sole representatives of the National
Council in the International. The Nation-
al Council of the United States will be rep-
resented in the International by Mrs. Fan-
nie Humphreys Gaffney of New York, its
president, and Miss Susan B. Anthony and
Rev. Anna Howard Shaw, the delegates
elect. Mrs. Hannah J. Bailey and Mrs.
Ellen A. Richardson are the alternates
elect for the two delegates above named.
In the presence of delegates alternates will
have no place in the International Council
sessions but that of observers.
It seems very important to emphasize 
these facts. I have received many letters
from different parts of the country from
local clubs, county and state societies, in-
forming me that “So and So have been
elected delegates to the International Coun-
cil,” and asking credentials. It is very
important that it be understood that of even 
NationalCouncils only those affiliated with an
International Council can send delegates to
this body. It is well known what National


[Column 1]
Organizations and what Local Councils are
affiliated with the National Council of the
United States. All members of such
National Organizations and such Local
Councils are, through the National Council
to which they belong, members also of the 
International Council; but with the excep-
tion of the women above named these are
not delegates, and their respective organiza-
tions may not independently send delegates
to the International Council.
Besides these regular official delegates the
International Council has invited fraternal
delegates from two classes of organizations:
From National Councils not yet affiliated
with the International Council, and from
International Organizations. So many
organizations are nominally international
that are really not so, that it is most im-
portant to be extremely careful, even in
establishing so slight a bond between the
International Council and the organization
as is implied in the invitation to the latter
to send a fraternal delegate. At the present
time the following organizations, with vary-
ing claims to a recognition of their Inter-
national scope, have been invited to send
fraternal delegates: The Arbitration and 
Peace Society; the Red Cross Federation;
the International Union of Friends of
Young Women; the International Associa-
tion for the Abolition of the State Regula-
tion of Vice; the World’s Woman’s Chris-
tian Temperance Union; the Young Wo-
man’s Christian Association, and the Gen-
eral Federation of Women’s Clubs.
It is probable that to this brief list the 
International Order of King’s Daughters
will be added.
It is important that it be understood that
the above organizations have no claim upon
the International Council, excepting that
of courtesy, and that their members, unless
related to the International Council through
their membership in some organization
affiliated with the National Council, are
eligible to apply for only the ordinary ticket
of admission to the Congress.
These statements are thus explicitly made
that there may be no embarrassment exper-
ienced later. It will certainly be under-
stood that they are not made because of
any desire on the part of the officers
of the International Council to ex-
clude desirable women from either Council
or Congress; on the other hand, the most
persistent, patient efforts have been made
during eleven years to diffuse an under-
standing of the organization of the National
Council and the International. The rela-
tion is so logical that the decisions made by
the Committee of Arrangements for the
Quinquennial meeting of the Council and
for the International Congress to be held in
connection with it and under its auspices,
must be regarded, not only just to the
organizations constituting the Council, but
very generous to the organizations still out-
side of the Council. 
III. What Americans will participate in
the Council, and what ones will take part
in the Congresses?
As voters there will sit in the Internation-
al Council, Mrs. May Wright Sewall,
vice-president at large; Mrs. Gaffney, Miss 
Anthony and Mrs. Shaw, in the capacities
above indicated, and only these. But as
participants in discussions, Mrs. Maria
Purdy Peck, vice-president of the National
Council, and Mrs. Emmeline B. Wells,
second recording secretary; with the follow-
ing patrons of the National Council: Miss

[Column 2]
Mary S. Anthony, Rochester; Miss Lucy
E. Anthony, Philadelphia; Mrs. Mary S.
Bybee, Indianapolis; Mrs. Ralph Emerson,
Rockford, Ill.; Mrs. Emily Cross, Chicago;
Mrs. Emma Shafter Howard, Oakland,
Cal.; Rev. Ida C. Hulton, Moline, Illinois;
Dr. Hannah E. Longshore, Philadelphia;
Mrs. Elizabeth Claridge McCune, Salt
Lake City; Mrs. Alice Wheeler Pierce,
Indianapolis; Mrs. Virginia T. Smith, Hart-
ford, and Mrs. Lucy B. Young, Salt Lake
City, are now expecting to be present.
Many of the above have also a part in
the formal program, as have also the fol-
lowing:
Miss Sadie American, Chicago; Mrs.
Cynthia Westover Alden, N. Y.; Miss
Octavia W. Bates, Detroit; Mrs. Harriet Santon
Blatch; Mrs. Isabelle C. Barrows; Mrs.
Margaret A. Caine, Salt Lake City; Mrs.
Lavina Doch, N. Y. City; Mrs. Sarah J.
Farmer, Greenacre, Elliott, Maine; Mrs.
Susa Young Gates, Provo City; Mrs. E. B.
Grannis, N.Y. City; Miss S. Goldmarck, N.
Y. City; Mrs. Ida Husted Harper, Washing-
ton, D. C.; Mrs. Lillian M. Hollister, De-
troit; Miss Emily Howland, Sherwood, N.
Y.; Mrs. Adelaide Johnson, Washington D.
C.; Mrs. Annie White Johnson, Chicago;
Mrs. Ellen C. Johnson, South Framingam,
Mass.; Miss Helen Kinne, Columbia Uni-
versity; Miss Klumpke, Cal; Mrs. Rebecca
D. Lowe, Atlanta, Ga.; Mrs. Mary F. Lov-
ell, Bryn Mawr; Miss Maxwell, Atlanta;
Mrs. Jenness Miller, N. Y.; Mrs. Frederick
Nathan, N. Y.; Miss Plummer, Mrs. Quan-
ter, Mrs. Isabella Hunter Robb, Cleveland;
Mrs. Cornelia S. Robinson, N. Y. City; Dr.
Sarah Hackett Stevenson, Chicago; Mrs.
Charlotte Perkins Stetson, Hartford; Miss
Mary Shaw, N. Y. City; Miss Emily Sar-
tain, Philadelphia; Miss Genevieve Ward,
N. Y. City; Dr. Gertrude Walker, Phila-
delphia.
Thus it will be seen that fifty-one of the
American women who are in some way
connected with the Council and the Con-
gress expect to be present.
When do Americans go? In reply to
this very frequent question, I must say that
no ship will carry all of them. Several are
already abroad; one small party of Ameri-
cans sailed on May 25th; another large
party with Miss Anthony as its leader on
June 3rd; a small party with Mrs. Sewall
on June 8th; the last with Mrs. Gaffney on
June 14th; and doubtless many are going on
other dates.
The correspondence which has resulted
in securing so large a representation of our
countrywomen in London has covered
many months. As the date approaches and
evidences of augmented interest multiply,
women who in the beginning were invited
to a place on the program are reconsider-
ing their earlier refusals. It is in no spirit
of tit for tat that the information must
frequently be given that the place declined
has been filled by some one else.
The Committee of Arrangements sitting
in London has done an enormous amount
of work. A copy of the minutes of each
session are in my hands, and they show
painstaking preparation and an elaboration
of detail which can be conceived only by
one with experience in such undertakings.
The latest notes reveal the greatest kind-
ness and zeal on the part of the Entertain-
ment and Hospitality Committees. The
list of entertainments contains the following:
(a) Reception and welcome of the In-
ternational delegates on Monday, June 26th,
at the Westminster Town Hall, which may

[Column 3]
be attended also by members of the Con-
gress.
(b) On the evening of the same day, a
reception at the Stafford House, where the
Duchess of Sutherland with the president
of the International Council, will receive
the guests. Holders of tickets for the
sectional meetings of the Congress (after
their names have been submitted to a sub-
committee on arrangements) may purchase
tickets for this reception at five shillings
each. Invitations will be issued to the In-
ternational delegates and invited speakers
in the name of the sub-committee of ar-
rangements.
(c) On the evening of Wednesday, June
28th, Lady Battersea will invite delegates
and speakers to Surrey House.
(d) On July 1st, the Bishop of London
and Mrs. Creighton will give a party at
Fulham Palace, to which members of the
Congress will be invited.
(e) On Tuesday, July 4th, Lady Roths-
child and Mrs. Leopold Rothschild will in-
vite members of the Congress to a garden
party at Gunnersburg Park.
(f) The President of the International
Council will entertain the delegates of the
Congress at a farewell gathering on the
evening of July 4th.
Besides the above large social entertain-
ments there are forerunners of numerous
more private hospitalities.
Through this public channel I beg to re-
mind the speakers that a typewritten copy
of their addresses, if not already in the
hands of the corresponding secretary, should
be sent at the earliest possible date to her
address, Miss Teresa F. Wilson, 9 Members
Mansion, Victoria St., S. W. London.
 The preparations are sufficiently advanced
to assure us that the second Quinquennial
of the International Council, and the Con-
gress held under its auspices, with all the
subsidiary events connected with it, will
constitute an opportunity for intellectual
and social experiences of a unique character.
American women who are to go abroad
this summer, whether related to the Coun-
cil or participants in the Congress, will
surely find that an attendance upon the
Congress will largely enhance the pleasure
of the ordinary visit to London.
The Hospitality Committee has had pre-
pared with great care a list of the hotels 
and boarding houses and lodging houses,
with a statement of cost and distance from
the seat of meeting, together with other in-
formation that will facilitate the ease of
getting settled upon arrival and of getting
about to the meetings. I have already
sent out to all who I know anticipate
going to London a parcel of the latest docu-
ments relating to the International Council,
together with forms of application for
tickets, and circulars of advice concerning
boarding houses. Large numbers of these
papers remain in my hands, and will be
sent gladly upon application to women de-
siring the information which they contain.
A rendezvous for American delegates, to the
Council and American members to the Con-
gress will soon be determined upon, and
the address may be obtained upon applica-
tion to me. All delegates, participants in
the program and members of the Congress
are desired upon arriving in London to
communicate their respective addresses to
the writer at the London address given be-
low. It will be my pleasure to give every
aid to my countrywomen in making the
Congress and its advantages accessible to
them. If this article should awaken in the


minds of its readers a question which it
does not answer, I shall gladly give further
information upon application.
			MAY WRIGHT SEWALL.
	Address to June 1st, 633 North Pennsyl-
vania St., Indiana.  After June 1st.  9 
Members' Mansion, Victoria Street, Lon-
don, England.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 
.


OBITUARY. 
Dear Aunt Rhoda, as we lovingly termed her, 
passed from death into eternal life at Provo, March
29, 1899; and while her body rests, peacefully in 
the silent tomb awaiting the resurrection of the 
just, her spirit is associating with friends and 
loved ones gone before. 
	Mother Young was born in Putnam Co., Tennes-
see, Oct. 24, 1820.  She became a member of the 
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in 
1842.  Moved to Nauvoo, where she became 
acquainted with the Prophet Joseph Smith. 
In 1852 she with her husband, Adolphin Young, 
started for Salt Lake City.  She lost her husband 
by death on the plains.  She afterwards married 
Alfred D. Young, the cousin of her former hus-
band.  They went to the Muddy, where they re-
mained until the mission was abandoned. They 
then moved to Kanab, where she made her home 
until the time of her death. 
	She was left a widow for the second time in 
1889. She was the mother of twelve children, 
and had at the time of her death seventy-five 
grandchildren and forty-nine great-grandchildrne.
	Sister Young was a faithful Latter-day Saint, 
and like a shock of wheat fully ripe, she has been 
garnered into her Father's house.  "Blessed are 
they who do hunger and thirst after righteousness, 
for they shall be filled." 
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas the Lord has called from our midst by 
the hand of death our beloved sister, 
RESOLVED, That we recognize in the death of 
our worthy sister the loss of a worthy member, 
and, when younger, an efficient teacher in the 
Kanab ward.
	RESOLVED, That we deeply sympathize with the 
bereaved family in the loss of a kind and affection-
ate mother. 
RESOLVED, That we esteem her past faithful 
labors as worthy the fellowship and highest respect 
of us all.
	RESOLVED, That these resolutions be put upon 
our records, that a copy of the same be presented 
to the family, and also to the WOMAN'S EXPONENT
for publication.
		ELIZABETH W. ROBINSON.
	The angel of death has visited another house in 
Kanab ward;  Elizabeth W. Robinson, wife of 
Bishop R. S. Robinson, passed peacefully away, 
Feb. 13, 1899.
	Sister Robinson was born at Staffordshire, Eng-
land, Nov. 5, 1835.  She emigrated to America in 
1842 and to Nauvoo in 1843; was driven with the 
Saints in 1846.  She came to Utah in 1849; was 
married to Richard S. Robinson; moved to Kanab 
in 1886, where her husband had been called to 
preside as Bishop.  Here she identified herself 
with the Relief Society and became an active 
member.  She was quiet and unassuming, yet 
kind and courteous to all.
	She was the mother of twelve children, and 
leaves thirty grandchildren and two great-grand-
children.  She had been a great sufferer for a 
long time when death kindly released her.  It can
truly be said of her:  "O, death! where is thy 
sting? O, grave! where is thy victory? Blessed 
are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth." 
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas an Allwise Providence has called from 
our midst by death our beloved sister, 
	RESOLVED, That we recognize in the death of our 
sister the loss of a worthy member, an affectionate 
teacher and a good Latter-day Saint, and worthy 
the respect of all. 
	RESOLVED, That we deeply sympathize with the 
bereaved family in the loss of a kind wife and 
affectionate mother.
	RESOLVED, That these resolutions be placed  upon 
our record and that a copy of the same be presen-
ted to the bereaved family, also to the WOMAN'S 
EXPONENT for publication.
M. E. LITTLE.


MODESTY in a woman is like color on her 
cheeks--very becoming if not put on.


PEACE MEETING, ORANGEVILLE.
	The ladies of this place joined their forces
in a peace meeting the evening of May 15th
according to request, and rendered the fol-
lowing programme:
	Congregational singing, “The Star Span-
gled Banner.” Prayer by the chaplain,
F. W. Young. Singing, “America.” The
“Appeal to Utah Women,”' in the Des-
eret News, read by Mrs. Maria Guymon.
An address by Mrs. Sarah A. Fullmer on
“The Importance of Arbitration.” A speech
by Mr. Jasper Robertson. A paper by J.
C. Childs on “The Importance of Peace.”
Song by Mrs. Maggie Cox and company,
“Break the News to Mother.” Speech by
J. K. Reid. Speech by B. F. Luke on
“The Educational Side for Peace.” Song
by Mrs. Hattie Reid and Mrs. Amanda Tut-
tle. Resolutions by Mrs. R. E. Robertson
Instrumental music by the Johnson band,
which was encored. Speech by U. E.
Curtis. Recitation by Kate Van Buren,
“The Returned Soldier.” Prayer by the
chaplain.
	The evening was a success. The hall
was artistically decorated with flags and
bunting, with an attentive audience that
joined in singing the closing piece, “Colum-
bia the Gem of the Ocean.”
					JOSIE E. CHILDS.


[Column 1]
R. S. REPORTS.
JOSEPA RELIEF SOCIETY.
	The Josepa Relief Society will have been
organized ten years next October. Sister
Emily G. Cluff was the first president,
with Sisters Kapukini and Pakekepa as her
counselors. Upon the release of President
H. H. Cluff on November 1, 1890, and
the appointment of Elder William King
his successor, Sister Lucy King succeeded
Sister Cluff, but in February, 1892, Brother
King died, and H. H. Cluff was again
called to preside over the colony, and there
followed the reappointment of Sister E. G.
Cluff to the presidency of the Relief Society,
which position she has continuously accept-
ed up to the present.
	The society has had a marked increase
numerically and financially since its or-
ganization, forty members are enrolled,
with an average attendance of thirty. By
gleaning in the wheat field and by dona
tion the society has accumulated forty
bushels of wheat. Members of the society
encouraged in gathering wheat desired to
have a piece of land and cultivate wheat
this summer, but upon the suggestion of
President Cluff the society have planted an
acre into melons, which have a ready sale
among the Hawaiians, and we anticipate
realizing $100.00 with which to buy wheat.
	The Relief Society has provided itself
with a full set of dishes for a banquet suf-
ficient to accomodate one hundred and
twenty-five persons. When used for the
feasts of the colony, other than the annual
feast gotten up by the society, a fee is
charged for the use of them, in addition to
replacing breakage and loss. The number
of annual feasts of the colony aggregate five
or six. When President Joseph F. Smith
and Elders visit the colony several times
during the year the Hawaiian people de-
light in entertaining them with feasts.
	At our recent conference here we were
visited by fourteen brethren and sisters.
Among the visitors were Sister Margaret
A. Caine, who came in the interest of seri-

[Column 2]
culture. Here she met Sister M. A. Till,
of Provo, who is on a visit, and who has
for some time recommended the silk indus-
try among this people, feeling they were
well adapted to it. She fully intended try-
ing to get it introduced among them while
out here. Sisters Till and Cluff had been
to the City and County Building to see the
silk exhibit, and to ask Mrs. Caine to send
out eggs and books of instruction, with the
intention of getting the Hawaiian sisters
started in raising cocoons. Meetings were
called to explain and get the sisters inter-
ested, and Mrs. Caine showed some native
silk fabric made in Utah, and gave such
information as would induce the sisters to
go into the enterprise. Elder Robert Tay-
lor, a missionary to the Islands, and who
had had some experierce with his mother
in raising cocoons when a boy, interpreted
for Mrs. Caine, and the progress in the first
effort of the Hawaiian sisters in raising silk
will be reported in due time. We already
have quite a number of mulberry trees to
start with.
	The colony anticipates a visit during the
summer from Queen Liliokalani, on her re-
turn from Washington to the Islands.
There are over one hundred of her people
in this colony. All appear happy and con-
tented, and enjoy having the EXPONENT
interpreted to them in the meetings of the
Relief Society, and hope it may be amply
supported.
EMILY G. CLUFF, Prest.,
ELLEN HALEMANU, Secy.

	A Peace Meeting was held in this colony
on the 15th of May. The Hawaiian
brethren and sisters expressed a desire for
peace upon the earth. The program con-
sisted of speeches, prayer and songs. They
all believe in settling difficulties by arbi-
tration rather than resort to war, and unite
in asking the leaders of nations to adopt the
plan proposed.
HANNA RAAEPA, Sec.
EMERY STAKE.
	The Emery Stake Relief Society Con-
ference convened at Ferron, April 21, 1899,
President Annie U. Larsen in the chair.
	After the usual opening exercises Sister
Larsen addressed the conference, bade all
welcome and felt glad to see the brethren
with us. Desired the sisters would exer-
cise their faith, that we might call down
the blessings of God upon us, that we may
have a good time together. She desired
the report of the presidents.
	Sister Swensen, president of Ferron, re-
ported the society of that place united and
willing to do what was required of them.
They had a society house nearly completed,
anticipated its completion this summer.
	Sister Jorgensen, Emery, reported her
society in a flourishing condition; all was
peace and harmony. They had one hun-
dred and seventy bushels of wheat, which,
according to the number of members, was a
good showing. The Bishop asked the sis-
ters to aid in building a meeting house.
	Sister Caroline Larsen, Castle Dale, re-
ported the society in a prosperous condition.
The sisters were feeling all right, and will-
ing to do what was required. They had
sixty bushels of wheat, a number of quitls
and considerable carpet-rags on hand. Gave
a good many notes from the General Relief
Society Conference at Salt Lake City in
April, also told how Sister Zina blessed all
the Relief Society. She was pleased to see
all the Stake presidency at the conference


and hoped we would be blessed with an
abundance of the Spirit of the Lord, which
she felt assured we would.
	Sister Robertson reported Orangeville
as doing well. Felt proud to report such a
noble band of sisters. They did a good
work in many ways and were making a
great effort to complete their house this
summer.
	The counselor of Molen reported their
society as doing well for so few in number.
They had donated to the Stake Academy,
and were ever willing to do what they
could at all times. There were only nine
branches reported in the statistical report
read by Sister Cox.
	President Larsen was pleased with the
reports of the society, especially with what
they had done for the Stake Academy, the
report showing that the amount of $55 00
had been given by the Relief Society of
Emery Stake. “Let us appreciate the
privileges we enjoy, and never forget our
prayers. We should cultivate punctuality
and a love for each other.”
	Sister Washburn reported Huntington,
felt all was well with the sisters, she knew
of few difficulties existing among them.
Hoped we would all remain faithful.
	Sister Childs reported Price, having been
there lately and being requested by the
president, Sister Pace, to do so should they
fail to send a representative. The sisters
at Price were doing very well, and in every-
thing were abreast with their sister towns.
They with others had donated liberally to
the Academy.
	Brother Lambert, of Salt Lake City, was
pleased to hear the reports[-] it was very
gratifying to him. Felt that we should all
live so that we could cultivate faith, as it
was a gift that was affected by the lives we
lived. Gave as an object lesson a circum-
stance that he was cognizant of, where
two families were living so near to the re-
quirements of the Lord that through the
faith they were able to exercise one of their
little ones was restored to life, that had
been dead for one and a half hours. Had
they not been living so perfectly their faith
would not have been equal for the unusual
emergency.
	The forenoon meeting was brought to a
close by the choir singing “We thank Thee,
O God, for a prophet,” etc.
MRS. STEVENS, Sec. pro tem.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.
	Semi-Monthly Meeting convened May
18, 1899, President M. I. Horne presiding.
Singing, “Where the voice of friendship’s
heard.” Prayer by Sister Julia C. Howe.
Singing, “Though nations rise.” Minutes
read and accepted.
	Sister L. D. Alder read the appeal made
to the women of Utah on the peace move-
ment.
	President Horne asked the sisters assem-
bled if they would like to turn this meeting
into a peace meeting. We as Latter-day
Saints always want peace, but we know
that in the fulfilling of prophecy there will
be contention. When we and all the world
are calling for peace, we will unite with all
to bring about peace as far as it is possible,
we will keep the commandments of God.
She knew that the prayers of the people
would result in good. Spoke of the mis-
sion of Jesus Christ, how the people doubted
His testimony, and how they also doubt
the same Gospel today. Spoke of the in-
fluence of women, even from the beginning

[Column 2]
of the world, and of the mission of Joseph
Smith and the persecution he was obliged
to endure. Wondered if all realize we are
so near to the end, and that, as the Savior
says, it will come suddenly.
	Sister L. D. Alder said she had been
visiting the Pocatello Stake in company
with Dr. Ellis R. Shipp, in regard to organ-
izing classes in obstetrics and nursing.
There was much need of such teaching,
there are so few obstetricians and the people
are so scattered, it is so hard upon the sick
as well as those who attend to them. Dr.
Shipp has organized classes and with ap-
parent success. Sister Alder expects to go
abroad before this meeting convenes again,
but asked the sisters to pray for her in her
absence. She has had the promise to go
and labor among her kindred. Thought
the peace meeting was an inspiration from
God.
	Sister Brown said “God moves in a
mysterious way.” She thought of all peo-
ple we were the most blessed; spoke of the
peace that Temple work brought to us, ex-
horted all who could to spend one day in a
month to go to the Temple and save souls.
	Counselor Elizabeth Stevenson said all
our meetings were peace meetings; only
the Latter-day Saints could enjoy this per-
fect peace. Spoke of the blessing derived
from reading the life of the Prophet Joseph
Smith, and interesting our children in his
mission, and of the waste of time in read-
ing light literature. Said we should all be
missionaries, not waste our time. Let us
all come here often and have our peace
meetings, and induce all to come. Said
that she appreciated the necessity of having
nurses trained to care for the poor and the
sick.
	Sister Martha Morris sang “O, Jesus the
Giver,” also spoke of the sacrifices the wo-
men of God would make. Spoke of the
testimonies she had been permitted to bear
to her kindred in the old country, and
prayed that Sister Alder would enjoy the
same blessing.
	Sister Phelps said we were serving a
God of power and revelation. Said we had
been begging for peace ever since the
Prophet Joseph Smith had organized the
Church, but nothing can thwart the pur-
poses of the Almighty, and He has said
that the wicked shall slay the wicked.
	Sister Brixen spoke of the importance of
teaching our children the ten command-
ments. Spoke of the great faith Moses
manifested, thought we should strive to
keep the commandments of God. Spoke
of the blessed time when we could live
upon the earth and say, “Thy will not
mine be done.”
	Sister Annie M. Cannon said we can
not expect universal peace over the earth,
but we can have peace by living the life of
a true Latter-day Saint. Highly approved
of the movement in training nurses all
over our State.
	Sister Susanna Fenamore spoke of her
desire for peace, and her love and desire to
work in the Temple.
	Sister Julia S. W[-]olley spoke of the
peace conference, and believed peace could
be brought about only by keeping the com-
mandments of God.
	Sister Julia C. Howe believed that the
predictions of prophets would come to pass,
that Christ was the author of peace, and
that Joseph the prophet was the forerunner
of the second coming of our Lord and
Savior. We must acknowledge the hand
of God in all things, we will be numbered

[Column 3]
among the jewels if we do our duty in
assembling ourselves together, etc. Spoke
of the necessity of teaching the children the
ten commandments and the keeping of the
Sabbath day.
	Adjourned for two weeks. Singing,
Doxology. Benediction by Counselor
Webb.
J. S. WOOLLEY, Sec.
