OUR GOD AND HIS PROPHET.

Our God—the God of Abraham,
   Isaac and Jacob, Prophets true;
The Great Omnipotent I Am:
   Let earth proclaim Thy praise anew!

Taking unto Thyself Thine own,
   Thy people mourned a Prophet dead;
Yet when twelve days had scarcely flown,
   Thou gav’st another in his stead.

Let Saints extol Thy name, and praise
   Thy matchless power to guide and bless;
Thy wond’rous work, in latter-days,
Thou cuttest short in righteousness.

Thy Prophet, as in days of old,
   Into Thy Temple dost repair:
Thy love doth marvelous light unfold,
   In answer to his fervent prayer.

With mighty faith he humbly kneels,
   To ascertain Thy holy will:
Thy grace the sovereign mind reveals;
   Thy promises Thou dost fulfill.

The vail ’twixt Thee and man withdrawn,
   Thy people understand and know,
In place of Wilford Woodruff, gone,
   Thou gavest them Lorenzo Snow.

By Joseph Smith and Brigham Young, 
   John Taylor, Wilford Woodruff, all,
He's nobly stood and labored 'mong
   Thy chosen people, great and small.

No truer friend hath Prophets known,
   No truer Prophet Saints need seek,
Than he to whom Thy mind is shown, 
   And by whose mouth Thy voice doth
	speak

One like to Moses, faithful proved,
   With sight undimmed and form erect, 
For more than eighty years, unmoved—
   Shall nations scoff? Will Saints reject?

"A full, free tithe the Saints must pay,
   From this time on”—God’s just demand:
Hear it, repeat it, and obey— 
   Ye Saints of God on every hand!

Thus doth the Prophet’s voice declare;
   So shall the Saints be justified;
Shake off the burdens, hard to bear,
   Of self-will, covetousness and pride.

Look to the God of Abraham,
   Ye Saints, and heed His Prophet’s voice;
Honor and serve the Great I Am,
   And in His covenant rejoice.

Spread Zion’s bound’ries far and wide,
   Her Center Stake redeem and build;
So shall our God be glorified, 
   His Prophet’s words be all fulfilled.

Let Zion’s children know and tell,
   Near and afar, where’er they go,
There is a God of Israel,
   Whose Prophet is Lorenzo Snow.
			   L. L. GREENE RICHARDS,
Sunday, July 2nd, 1899.


[Column 1]
WOMEN'S INTERNATIONAL COUNCIL.
	The reports of the Women’s International
Council which have reached this country are
meagre and inadequate, but it is clear that the
Council was a success, both from the exul-
tation of its friends and the growls of its
enemies. A correspondent of the N. Y.
Tribune says:
	"London has offered many unique attrac-
tions for the throngs of visitors this week.
The Woman’s International Council, with
scores of famous women from Europe,
America and the colonies, has been a re-
markable tournament of wit, wisdom and
tact. The House of Lords threw down the
gauntlet in ungallant style on the opening
day by amending the London bill with a
view to shutting women out of the new
Metropolitan Councils. The action of
these three hundred peers, who only turn
out for full dress parade in order to snub
women, frown upon deceased wives’ sis-
ters and affront Erin, has not interfered
with the Council’s leisurely and contempla-
tive rambles in every field of discussion
under the sun. With more than fifty
meetings and the best platform talkers of
the sex to address the delegates, this parlia-
ment of women has been a triumph of or-
ganization, moderation and good sense. If
it has done nothing else, it has offered con-
vincing proof of the remarkable talent of
women for public speaking and the wide
range of their interests under modern con-
ditions of education.
	"Apart from the passage of a resolution in
favor of arbitration, the Council has not
taken definite action on any subject, and
Lady Aberdeen’s presidential address sug-
gested no other motive for the assembly
than the Golden Rule of love, which was a
generalization sufficiently broad for a
World’s Congress, but was neither sensa-
tional nor revolutionary.
	"Lady Aberdeen has been the most con-
spicuous figure of the Congress, presiding
with dignity and grace. Miss Anthony
has excited more enthusiasm than any
other speaker, and the American delegates
generally have done the best work. Among
these are Mrs. Charlotte Perkins Stets[-]n,
Miss Genevieve Ward, Mrs. Cynthia West-
over Alden, Miss May Shaw, Mrs. May
Wright Sewall, Mrs. Fannie St. J. Gaffney
and the Rev. Anna Howard Shaw. The
most interesting meeting was that presided
over by Mrs. Kendal, when the drama as a
field for women was considered. Mrs.
Flora Annie Steel, Mrs. Kate Douglas
Wiggin and Lady Lindsay were the chief
figures at a similar meeting under the
head of literature. The delegates have
been entertained at Stafford House, Fulham
Palace and many private houses."
	The London Society of American Wom-
en gave a luncheon at the Hotel Cecil in
honor of the American delegates on July 3.
Mrs. Hughes Griffin, the president of the
society, occupied the chair, flanked by
Lady Aberdeen, the president of the Wom-
en’s International Council, on the right,
and Mrs. Choate, wife of the ambassador,
on the left. The tables were decorated
with red, white and blue flowers, and

[Column 2]
American and British flags were displayed
behind the chair. There were 250 persons
present, all of them women except the
brother of Miss Leonora Jackson, the
violiniste, who plays accompaniments.
	Mrs. Hughes Griffin gave an address of
welcome to the American delegates, and
Lady Aberdeen, on behalf of the Women's
International Council, thanked the Ameri-
can women for the help and inspiration
they had given to women in general. "We
are indebted to the American women," she
said, "for many grand ideas. Much of the
increased brightness of our lives is due to
the fact that it is now granted that women
have a sense of humor."
	Mrs. May Wright Sewall, vice-president
of the Council, said that America was be-
coming a country which, by the necessity
of its institutions and relationships, must
move toward internationalism. Mrs.
Charlotte Perkins Stetson and Mrs. Jennie
C. Croly also spoke.
	At one meeting of the Council, which
was devoted to agriculture, Mrs. Alexander
Tweedle presided. Prof. Robertson, of
Canada, said he considered agriculture a
most suitable occupation for women. Lady
Vernon said that dairying was pre-eminent-
ly woman’s work. She urged that atten-
tion be given by women to butter-making
in winter and cheese-making in summer.
Mrs. Virginia C. Meredith, of the School of
Agriculture at the Minnesota State Uni-
versity, advocated stock breeding for wom-
en. Mme. Delen, of Bruges, spoke on the
women’s agricultural work in Belgium.
Mrs. Margaret A. Caine gave an exposition
of silk culture as an occupation for women,
and Mrs. Alexander described ostrich
farming by women in New Zealand.
	At the meeting devoted to women fac-
tory inspectors, the Hon. Mrs. Lyttleton
presiding, a paper by Mrs. Carlyle, of
Canada, was read by Mrs. Carty. It
showed the strides made in Canada in re-
gard to women inspectors and labor legisla-
sion in general.
	"The Scientific Treatment of Domestic
Servants," was another subject discussed.
Mrs. Gates, speaking of the necessity of
training the lady of the house as well as
the servants, complimented the Queen,
and declared that "this queenly woman
and womanly Queen" had had an influence
upon women as mistresses of servants as
well as in other relations of life.
	Dr. Garnett, who presided over the con-
ference of women librarians and indexers,
said that England was far behind America
in paying women librarians.
	Countess Bective presided over the meet-
ing which discussed the aptitude of women
in handicrafts.
	On the afternoon of July 4 the delegates
were entertained at a garden party by Lady
Rothschild at Gunnesbury Park, and in
the evening the Countess of Aberdeen gave
them a farewell reception.—Ex.


KNOWLEDGE is better that wealth, yet
how many people there are in this wicked
world who prefer the inferior article.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
LADIES' SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	The Ladies' Semi-Monthly Meeting of
June 10, 1899, convened in the 14th ward
hall at 2 o'clock, President M. I. Horne
presiding.
	Meeting opened by singing, "We thank
Thee, O God, for a prophet," Prayer by
Sister Margaret P. Young. Singing,
"Welcome best of all good meetings."
Minutes of last meeting read and approved.
	President Horne said she was pleased to
see so many present; that to come together
to testify of the goodness of God, was food
to our spirits. We should set an example
worthy of patronage before our children.
Spoke of the trials that were before us all,
for this is a day of proving. Spoke of the
trials of the early days in Utah, and of the
thankfulness of our hearts that we could
get away from wicked and designing men.
Exhorted us all to train our children by
attending to our duties as we wish them to
do. Spoke of the word of the Lord to 
President Snow, that the people should pay
a full tithing, and of the blessings we
would receive if we would keep the com- 
mandments of God. These commandments
must be kept if we expect to get the bless-
ings. Referred to the great amount of tea
and coffee used in our State, and said how
wrong it must look to our Heavenly Father
that we cannot let these injurious articles
alone. Said she had heard President Young
say that "those who cannot keep the Word
of Wisdom, cannot go to help build up
Jackson County."
	Sister Barnes said she had been a long
while in the Church, but she knew there
was great room for improvement; that the
Relief Society would grow and its good
work increase.
	A sister in the congregation said she be-
lieved in the law of tithing, and that we
would all be blessed in observing this law.
Another sister spoke in tongues.  Sister
Zina D. H. Young gave the interpretation.
	The congregation then sang "Come let
us anew."
	Sister Julia C. Howe said those who are
raised up to lead us are in the right place.
She had always looked upon the law of
tithing as a test of fellowship; we should
teach our children to pay tithing. Spoke
of Daniel's faith, and the wisdom he gained
by refusing the food from the king's table
and observing the laws of nature. Spoke
of the trial it had been to her to stand up
and bear her testimony, but how she had
been greatly blessed in overcoming this
spirit.
	Sister  Liddle spoke of the blessings she
had received from the Lord and the bless-
ings for observing the law of tithing.
	Sister Brown bore a testimony to the law
of tithing and keeping the Sabbath day.
	Sister Barnes, of the 11th ward, knew
that we were richer when we paid our
tithing, we should always pay the best we
have. She had followed out the plan, and
she knew the Lord had blessed her for it.
	Sister Emily Stevenson spoke of the
blessing of Relief Society work, what a
benefit it is to the poor and sick and
afflicted, and the faithful workers that
would no doubt receive a reward.
	A sister in the congregation bore testi-
mony to the law of tithing. Spoke of how
the adversary will take advantage of us
when we neglect little duties.
	Sister Sanders said she had received
many testimonies in this house. Spoke of

[Column 2]
the blessings derived in observing the law
of tithing.
	Sister Smith said she could bear a testi-
mony that the spirit of God was here; also
spoke of the blessing derived from paying
tithing.
	Sister Isaacs knew that her Father heard
and answered prayers. Would rather go
without a great deal and pay an honest
tithing.
	Sister Wright said she always paid her
tithing, and thanked her Father for His
blessings.
	Sister Haite spoke of the glorious work
we are engaged in. Always felt blessed in
paying tithing and donations. Said she
was always pleased to present the Gospel
to those who would accept it.
Sister Bateman said she knew this was
the church of God, and thanked Him for
the privileges she had, for the Gospel and
the privilege of working in the Temple,
etc.; also testified to the truthfulness of the
law of tithing.
	Sister Cooper said she had always paid
an honest tithing and testified to the truth
of the work.
Sister Julia S. Woolley bore testimony
of the law of tithing and standing firm for
the truth.
	Sister Parry spoke of tithing and the
blessings obtained by keeping the com-
mandments of God.
	A sister in the congregation spoke of the
spirit she had been privileged to enjoy,
and said she knew it was proper to pay
an honest tithing. Spoke of the testimony
of a young man, who said that he owed
his success in life to paying an honest tith-
ing.
	Sister Horne said the Lord knows our
weakness, He will be our judge. Spoke of
the sin in breaking the Sabbath day, and
the temptations that were held out to spend
this day in amusements.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Sing-
ing, Doxology. Benediction by Sister
Zina D. H. Young.

The Semi-Monthly Meeting convened as
usual June 24, 1899 in the 14th ward hall
at 2 o'clock. Sister Bathsheba W. Smith
called the meeting to order. Singing,
"For the strength of the hills we bless
Thee." Prayer by Sister Julia Howe.
Singing, "O, say what is truth." Minutes
of previous meeting read and approved.
	Sister Bathsheba W. Smith said she was
pleased to be present, and felt that this
meeting was a school for the sisters; that
the Lord was always present; that we
must be faithful and true to enable us to
cope with evil influences. Thought that
outside of the sisters engaged in this work
so much love did not exist.
	Sister Julia C. Howe said she thought it
very necessary to have our memories re-
freshed often in regard to doing our duty;
said she felt to honor her parents for teach-
ing her to honor God. She was taught the
scriptures, and when she heard the Gospel
she was prepared to accept it. She thought
it was necessary to have humility in order
to understand the Gospel. Referred to the
humility the Prophet Joseph Smith ex-
pressed in his prayer to the Lord the first
time he went into the woods to pray.
	Sister Brown thanked the Lord that He
had given her a knowledge of the Gospel
and permitted her to work in the Temple.
Said she believed firmly in the law of tith-
ing, and spoke of the good spirit that she

[Column 3]
had always enjoyed at these meetings.
	Counselor Stevenson said she had always
been an attendant at these meetings. She
thought it was necessary for the sisters to
come together often and better prepare
themselves to perform their mission in life.
	Sister Margaret Goddard said she was
thankful that her parents were God-fearing
people.
	Sister Barton said she had always be-
lieved in and practiced the law of tithing.
She knew the Lord had sent many bright
spirits in this age to fill the responsible
positions.
	Sisters Munn, Badger and Waterfall bore
faithful testimonies to the truth of the
work we are engaged in.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks.
					J. S. Woolley, Sec.


OLD FOLKS' DAY AT PAROWAN.
	Parowan, July 3, 1899.
Editor Woman's Exponent:
	Under the auspices of the Relief Society
of this ward, "Old Folks' Day" was cele-
brated on the 29th of June; and we believe
our aged fathers and mothers will long
cherish the remembrance of that worthy
event.  At 2 p. m. a feast of choice variety
was enjoyed;  immediately after a creditable
program was rendered, which seemed to
immensely please those very aged and en-
feebled, who seldom get out to public
gatherings.
	Badges were presented by the Relief
Society designating the time spent by the
wearer in this estate.  Each furrowed
countenance would involuntarily relax into
a smile as the souvenir was fastened upon
his breast, so gratified were they at every
recognition of their old age. 
	At the close of the program, cake and 
lemonade were served, when all those
honored veterans were treated to a carriage
drive.  As usual in our little country
towns the affair was finally crowned with a 
ball in the evening, where refreshments
were again in order.  All over sixty were
given complimentary tickets, and it was
really surprising to see how many who had
passed the third score milestone were
yet able to enjoy the pleasures of the ball-
room.
	Thus ended a happy day, one that had 
created new joys in many hearts who had
perhaps thought they were "unhonored
and unsung." Truly we cannot devote
too much time in making happy days for
our aged ones.  There are none who better
appreciate our efforts, and none to whom
we owe a greater debt. 
				E. CRANE WATSON.


As a result of a series of divisions on the
London government bill in the English
House of Commons recently, it was de-
cided by 179 votes to 77 that women should
not be eligible to act as mayors of London.
The question whether women should be
qualified to sit as councilors was deferred,
and will probably be decided in favor of the
women. Women are already serving ac-
ceptably as county councilors in other
parts of Great Britain, and there seems no
reason why they should be excluded in
London. The men on the London County
Council wish for the co-operation of women,
and petitioned Parliament some ten years
ago to let women serve.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE VOICELESS SYLLABLES OF GRASS.
	(In Sea Drift, a just issued volume of poems by
Grace Ellery Channing, occur these stanzas on
Walt Whitman:)
	O'er Tuscan hills I heard it breathed,
And up the olive slopes it ran, 
And then the laurel all began
Singing of brows once laurel wreathed;
	Of bards whose vanished voiced fill 
The resonant, deep valleys yet;
The laurel never can forget,
Such echoes tremble through it still.
	But I—I let the laurel pass,
And pass, the dim Etrurian land;
Far louder sing within my hand 
The voiceless syllables of grass.
	Your music keeps its mighty ring, 
O ancient groves of Tuscany !
But tenfold eloquent to me
The common herb One taught to sing.
	Green art thou, laurel overhead,
Yet somber to this tiny blade,
And some one says that he who made 
The grass and me to live, is dead.
	It may be true—for Italy
Hath seen the night of many a sun;
Thou, O my Country, hadst but one,
If set, how dim thy light must be!
	It may be false—the sky’s as blue,
The ilex has not dropped a leaf 
Nor earth put off a rose for grief,
And yet, for all—it may be true.
	Unruffled, silver olives wave,
Loud sings the laurel where I pass,
But louder still I hear the grass— 
The grass, new growing, on a grave.
							T.


[Column 1]
AN INTERESTING LETTER.
			
Butte City, Montana, June 20, 1899.

Editor Woman's Exponent:

	Agreeable to promise to write to you
from this place, I now undertake to do so.
	I left Salt Lake City on the O. S. L.
9:45 a. m. train, May 16th, for this place,
in response to a telegram informing me of
the serious illness of my granddaughter,
and requesting me to come.
	It being my first trip out of Utah since I
entered it a little child in 1848, I did not
much like the idea of starting out under
such distressing circumstances; but ‘‘neces-
sity knows no law,” and in response to
the call of distress from her children, a
mother will not stop to discuss the order of
her going, but go.
	The journey was accomplished without
any incident worthy of mention, except the
extreme anxiety of mind which I endured
concerning the condition of my grand-
daughter: whether I should find her dead
or alive at my journey’s end.
	The beginning of the journey being
over roads which I had traveled before,
nothing new presented itself to my view
until I reached Pocatello, where a short
stop occurred. My impression of the
town, from what I could see, was a long
row of shops, stores, etc., with scarcely any
residences in sight, which made me won-
der where all the people lived who patron-
ized them. Then on, over hills and rough
places, going after dark through places
that were new to me, so that I was pre-
vented from seeing much that was really
new. 
	Arriving at my destination at 1:05 a. m.
of the 17th, I was met by my grandson
with a carriage and taken to 206 W. Mer-
cury St., where I found my little grand-
daughter out of danger, thanks to our
Heavenly Father, who honored the ad-
ministration of His servants, Elder Chris-

[Column 2]
tian Williardson, Andrew King and Charles
O. Dickson, who had been constant in
their attendance upon her before I came,
and the care of an excellent physician.
	But with her recovery our troubles were
not over. On the day of her leaving her
bed my daughter was stricken down, and
the doctor, after working with her for a
time,told us that she was in a dying condi-
tion and had not probably over two hours to
live, and asked if we wished to call in any
one else. We told him that we had all the
confidence in him that we could have in
any physician, but I said to him: ‘‘There
is One who can do what you cannot do,
and He is God, and His authorized ser-
vants are here, and He will honor them.”
He said: “All right, if you can do anything
you had better do it quick.”
	We sent for the Elders, who came and
anointed her with oil and laid hands upon
her, and she revived immediately. And
when the doctor came in the next morning,
he said he had never seen anything like it
before in his life, her pulse and tempera-
ture being perfectly normal and she prop-
ped up in bed smiling and looking well.
	Then began a struggle which lasted
about a week, in which she suffered two or
three relapses, when it seemed as though
death would claim the victory. But the
Elders stood by us nobly day and night, as
many as five of them surrounding her bed
in circle prayer when she seemed to be dy-
ing, after which she would revive and ap-
pear to be getting well; and at last, I am
happy and thankful to our Heavenly Fath-
er to be able to say, she is on the road to
complete recovery, although at the end of
two weeks of illness she could not walk one
step.
	I am not able, under the circumstances
which have prevented me from going out
much, to tell you much about Butte, ex-
cept what I have observed from our front
windows, which, by the way, are very
favorably located for the purpose in some
respects.
	There are three things that I have
been able to see without going abroad:
	First: All the funerals from the Catholic
Church (and Butte is principally Catholic)
have to pass our house on their way to the
cemetery, and in consequence we see from
one to five almost every day, anywhere
from one block to one mile in length; and
as the service only occupies about ten min-
utes, there is no tedious waiting one for
another, and we sometimes see three or
four within an hour; and once we saw a
double funeral of two miners who were
killed: two hearses and two long rows of
carriages walking abreast. It was quite a
solemn sight.
	Second: The cripples who daily pass the
house on their way to the hospital around
the corner, their limbs gone, limping,
walking on crutches, heads bandaged, etc.
	Third, last but not least by any means:
The throngs that go to the Catholic church
from 6 a. m. until 12 m. on Sunday, there
being a separate mass every hour, and a
new crowd at each one. Every Sunday
forenoon it looks like our conference at
home.
	One week ago last Sunday we saw fifty
or more little girls going to their first com-
munion, all dressed as brides, in white,
with long veils and orange blossoms; in-
deed they are called the brides of Christ.
I regretted that I had not the opportunity
of witnessing the ceremony.
	The little branch of the Church of Jesus

[Colum  3]
Christ of Latter-day Saints here in Butte
numbers about sixty, and is ably presided
over by Brother Christian Williardson, of
Ephraim, Sanpete Co., who, with Elders
Andrew King, Jr., Hyrum M. Larsen and
Sorenson of Cache Co., and Charles O.
Dickson of Provo, Utah, form a power
here for good in spreading the principles of
the Gospel and administering in the or-
dinances thereof.
	They have a Sunday School and Relief
Society, the latter presided over by Sister
Nellie Yanney, with Sister Andrews and
Sister Sarah Larson as counselors, Sister
Larson having been set apart last Sunday
evening. Sister Watt is secretary of the
society.
	The Elders’ headquarters and place for
meetings, socials, etc., is at 902 Utah Ave.,
S. Butte.
	I have had the privilege of meeting with
the Saints in their regular Sunday after-
noon and evening meetings, also Relief
Society meetings, and have enjoyed the
good influence which prevails there, for I
know that it is of God; and I regret that I
did not have the opportunity of meeting
with them upon their festive occasions,
which occur once a week in the form of a
social party.
	The birthday anniversary of President
Young was duly celebrated, and the Relief
Society will in a few days celebrate their
first anniversary.
	Well,although I did not get to see much,
I might occupy more space in describing
my impressions of people and things that
I have met, but my letter is already per-
haps too lengthy, so I will close with kind
love and a hope of seeing you in the near
future.
		Your sister,,
			CAROLINE E. R. WELLS.


TRIBUTE TO OUR WOMEN.
	A representative of the Associated Press
interviewed the Countess of Aberdeen and
others regarding the practical results likely
to accrue from the international council of
women. Lady Aberdeen, who is an en-
thusiastic admirer of the American and
Canadian delegates, said:
	“Every one of them was a skilled ex-
pert and most of them were trained speakers
whom the congress was delighted to hear,
which cannot be said in regard to many of
the other speakers. The Americans, as a
rule, were better informed, more decided in
opinion and further advanced on the lead-
ing topics than any of the other delegates. 
They fairly outstripped the English wom-
en, who, however, were better informed
than the continental delegates in general
knowledge of the subjects pertaining to
women.
	“Mrs. Sewall won the congress from the
first. She has extraordinary tact in deal-
ing with such a large gathering of repre-
sentative women.
	“Miss Susan B. Anthony’s powerful ad-
vocacy of women’s claims distinctly added
to her already great reputation both at
home and abroad.
	“Regarding the results, the grand con-
summation has been reached of welding
together a cosmopolitan body of thoughtful
women, prepared to educate their fellow
women. Twenty-five years hence the
leaven of today will have leavened the
whole world, and the irresistible force of
public opinion will have raised women to
their proper sphere.”

SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JULY 15, 1899.

[Column 2]
WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


(Column 1)
CHARITY AND LABOR.
	The same sentiment or attribute so
divinely portrayed by the Apostle Paul in
his first Epistle to the Corinthians, 13th
chapter, which he calls Charity, Henry
Drummond, in his master lecture, “The
Greatest Thing in the World,” designates
as “Love.” That definition of Drum-
mond’s is singularly appropriate and ac-
ceptable; all the more so because an older
writer and greater prophet than he defines
the same thing, thus: “But charity is the
pure love of Christ and it endureth for-
ever.”—Book of Mormon, Moroni, 7th chap.
	The Great Father exercised this charity,
or love, toward His children in the begin-
ning of their career upon this earth, in a
very different way to that in which many
fond parents manifest what they under-
stand to be the most tender love for their
offspring, in these later times. This earth
was created for Adam and his generations
after him. And the Garden of Eden was
planned and planted for him and Eve to
make their home in. But their Father did
not say to them, “My son and my daugh-
ter, this beautiful home is for you to take
your ease and pleasure in, unburdened
with cares and labors, such as your mother
and I have borne in making it for you.”
Genesis 2nd chapter and 15th verse tells us
that “The Lord God took the man and put
him into the Garden of Eden, to dress it
and to keep it. ”
	God’s great love for His son Adam made
Him considerate of his welfare to the ex-
tent that He gave him work to do which
should claim his time and attention and
keep him from running after idlers and
mischief-makers who might be allowed to
find their way into his garden and seek to
decoy him from the path of duty. And
not only did Adam’s Father furnish him
with employment for his hands, but He
had also a tremendous amount of brain
work laid out for him as well. He was
expected to find names for all the beasts of
the fields and fowls of the air: business
enough to keep him actively engaged for
quite a length of time, we would think in
these days.
	But according to modern notions, al-
though Eve received the distinguished and
important appointment of help-mate to
Adam, yet upon her was placed no special
responsibility, except in the first great
commandment which charged the man and
his wife to be fruitful and multiply, and

(Column 2)
replenish the earth. Eve’s life in Eden,
previous to the step which led to the
banishment of herself and husband, has
been regarded in a light akin to the follow-
ing:

In the beautiful garden of Eden,
	Ere the fall of the woman and man, 
Eve must have had a good time—
	Imagine it once, if you can.

Birds in the tree-tops singing,
	Or soaring in balmy air,
Could not be freer than she was,
	From burdens of household care.

No dishes to wash after breakfast,
	No planning of what to have next,
For luncheon or dinner or supper,
	No man disappointed and vexed.

The most luscious fruits always ready,
	Adam would have but to say,
And Eve would have only to gather,
	Whatever they wanted that day.

No ironing, washing or mending, 
	The buttons all torn off and lost;
No household accounts to examine,
	With hopes of reducing the cost.

But, alas, how the tempter beguiled her,
	When he found her with nothing to do!
What a warning to all of her children,
	Reaching even to me and to you.

That too much of wealth and of leisure
	Are not the best things to receive,
Is shown by the record that's given
	To us of our great Mother Eve.

	That all honest industry is honorable, is
a truth too old and too obvious to require
argument. And yet to earn a living by
laborious industry seems to be as much
against the nature of people in general, be-
cause it is erroneously considered more re-
spectable to be able to live without work
than by it, as that it requires self-sacrifice
in some respects to devote time and energy
to industrial pursuits. But there are many
sides to this question of charity and labor.
	While there are those who would pre-
fer to live by the sweat of others’ brows
than their own, there are, on the other
hand, hard-working and generous-hearted
people, who would rather be over-taxed
themselves, for the sake of earning and
handling more money, even though they
divide their earnings with their poorer
neighbors, than to share their work with
them. This, is mistaken kindness, false
charity, and works injury to both parties
concerned. Would it not be far better if
the wise financiers, in our communities
would lend their energies and means to the
systematic arrangement of industries in
which all might find lucrative employment,
instead of the few building up themselves,
being over-worked because of their anxiety
to earn and save, even though they give
liberally of their substance to feed the poor?
Certainly it is better to teach children to
work and earn at least a portion of what
they need and want, than to give them all
they require, demanding nothing in return.
And the same rule will apply to men and
women as well as boys and girls.
	In our charitable institutions, therefore,
evidently the wisest thing to do would be
to create industries in which all who are
able to work might be happily employed,
being kindly encouraged to do their best to
earn their living. Instructions to this effect
were given by the great organizer of the
Relief Society among the Latter-day Saints,
the Prophet Joseph Smith, more than fifty
years ago.
	Great care and prudence would neces-
sarily have to be exercised in the distri-
bution of labor among the poor. A feeble,
delicate woman would not be expected to

(Column 3)
wash and scrub, as a strong, well one
might. But the former would perhaps be
able to braid straw, darn or patch light
material, and find relief in some such easy
work, which idleness could never bring.
And the strong one would probably sing
over her heavier tasks, taking satisfaction
in them which she would never discover in
lighter employment.
	How many there are around us who are
needing to have their work thus planned
out and arranged for them! And how
much happier and more prosperous the
world might be made if the wise managers
would generously and patiently give some
of their time and skill to the instruction of
others on these points; and if those who
are less gifted would be humbly teachable,
and strive to learn! God speed the time
when selfishness shall no longer exist,
when each shall love his neighbor as him-
self, when there shall be no poor, for all
shall be rich in the peaceful enjoyment of
true charity and satisfactory labor!
	L. L. GREENE RICHARDS.


TO SISTER ELIZABETH STEVENSON.

“Leaves have their time to fall,
And flowers to wither at the north wind’s breath;
And stars to set—but all—
Thou hast all hours for thine own, oh death !”

	On Friday morning, June 30th, 1899, at
8 o’clock, as the sister workers of the Salt
Lake Temple entered their weekly meeting,
a great solemn hush of more than usual
significance came over them. Sisters Zina
D. H. Young, Bathsheba W. Smith and
Emma Woodruff, (the presidency of the
sisters in the Temple,) were there in their
accustomed places. And nearly all the
workers were present, but Sister Elizabeth
Stevenson was missing. The solemnity
which characterized the opening of the
meeting, and could scarcely be accounted
for in full, continued until its close, when
one announced that as she entered the
house the news had been given to her
that Charles Stevenson was dead! Then a
special prayer for Charlie’s motherwent up.
from that band of earnest, faithful workers
They prayed with loving fervor for every
comforting blessing to be bestowed upon
their bereaved sister, and dropped tears of
true sympathy which only those who have
themselves known sorrow could shed.
	Sister Elizabeth, if there can be any con-
solation in the knowledge that your sister
workers mourn with you the early loss of
your bright, gifted, noble boy, then be
thou comforted.

	“True until death! can more be said ?
	Unto all those who weepeth,
	May we not say of the ‘Blessed dead,’
	He is not dead, but sleepeth?’
	Sleepeth to waken, fair and young,
	When the song of the blest redeemed is sung.”
								    LULA.


WE gratefully acknowledge the gift of
some beautiful flowers from Mrs. Dow,
wife of the warden of the penitentiary.
They were very much appreciated and ad-
mired, and cast quite a cheerful influence
throughout our office.
	ABOUT 2,000 of our old people spent the
day at Capt. Dallin’s popular resort in Utah
Co., on Friday, July 7.  The R. G. W.
Railway Company furnished two fine cars
free of expense.  The resort was crowded
to its fullest capacity, and the time was
pleasantly spent in visiting, picnicing and a
short program of sports.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]		
		A WOMAN MISSIONARY.
   [Mrs.] Addison Pratt on the Society Islands,
1850.
			[Continued.]
	The 10th day of July. Roads unaccount-
able: up and down like the affairs of life. Met
traders with flour and groceries for the poor
emigrants. The men went out in search of
gold; found enough to buy some wine and
brandy to rejoice over the event of getting
safely over the last mountain. We shouted
glory hallelujah! Some of the crowd
thought as did the father of the prodigal
son: ‘‘It was meet that we should make
merry and rejoice.” They wanted a
Jubilee. 
	The 13th day. The horn sounded for
preaching; the people assembled, prayed
and sang hymns.
	Now we are fairly in the gold country—
a land of desolation, a barren desert. The
poor diggers look forlorn. Behold two
men I saw from the state of Illinois, from
the immediate vicinity of my brother, whom
I have not seen for many years! They
knew him, and could inform me of his
welfare. I have been truly homesick to-
day: May the Lord speed us on to our
place of destination. Oh, what a dismal
country ! No feed, no green thing grow-
ing—nothing but gold!
	The 16th day. Camped in an oak grove
near Brown’s, a fictitious name for Porter
Rockwell. Found a spring of clear, cold
water.
	The 17th day. Today arrived Brothers
Amasa Lyman and C. C. Rich, whom we
were most happy to meet. They brought
me a letter from my husband, dated April
24th, the same day he set sail at San Fran-
cisco for the Islands. The heat is almost
insupportable. Several old friends who
came to this country before us, today called
to see us. Men are reckless in this country;
there is great want of woman’s influence.
Filth and confusion throughout the whole
country. Sister Caroline Crosby and I
visited Brown’s and Steward’s. They
treated us kindly and made us presents.
They make money by selling liquors and
provisions to the emigrants. To be obliged
to see drunkenness and listen to profane
language to earn a livelihood, and that
without cessation, how can they endure it!
Preaching today by Brothers Rich and
Lyman. To see their faces and hear their
voices proclaiming the truth so far from
home, is comforting to the soul!
	The 23rd day. Camped six miles from
Sacramento. The public buildings are
made of cloth. Brother Crosby sold his
oxen and wagon for $225.00
	July 24th. One year ago today we at-
tended the great celebration in Salt Lake
Valley. Oh, we hope they will remember
us today, and drink us a toast[-] We are
camped on the north fork of the Sacramento
river; water clear and soft. The brethren
who went back to the mountains after ice
and snow for Stewart’s market, have just re-
turned tired, themselves and teams worn
out. The price has fallen from one dollar
to five cents per pound.
	The 25th. Came down to the great city
that has made such a noise in the world. A
great city it is, for the age of it, but so
filthy it is dangerous for people to stop
there.
	July 26th. Pitched our tents on the
bank of the Sacramento river, unloaded
our wagons and the men who had pur-
chased them took them away. I saw my

[Column  2]
faithful old carriage go, which for four years
had been a sleeping room for my children;
besides I had traveled in it two thousand
miles. It was the last piece of property
that remained of all the labors of my youth,
and that of my husband. I received only
thirty dollars for it, as it was badly worn.
The heat was intolerable. Not a tree to
shield us from the scorching sun. We
were preparing to go on board the bark
Alden, Capt. Alden, bound for San Fran-
cisco. Soon after we unloaded our goods
several gentlemen called, to welcome us
with acts of politeness. They brought in
a variety of refreshments, nice drinks and
pastry. It was thankfully received, weary
and worn as we were.
	A man from St. Louis called, who had
married a particular friend of ours in Nau-
voo. He informed us that his wife’s
mother had died of cholera at his residence
in St. Louis; that he had sent his wife with
two children to Nauvoo, hoping they might
escape that malignant disease.
	Joy and sorrow go hand in hand: we rejoice
to meet old friends, sorrow at parting. Our
traveling companions who were destined for
the mines called to bid us good bye. They
looked sad at the sight of the vessel we had
to embark in. The bark was large and
convenient, but oh, so dirty ! We went
on board, chose our berths, expecting to
have a good night’s rest. No sooner had
we laid our heads on our pillows than the
mosquitos commenced their ravages! Like
a mob of armed men they invaded our
quarters. We all abandoned the cabin and
fled on deck; but all in vain, we could not
elude their vigilance. Two-thirds larger
than insects of the common class, their bites
were like the sting of bees. I wrapped myself
in a thick blanket and sat in my chair. In
spite of all my efforts to cover myself they
pierced me till my feet and ankles became
swollen and inflamed. I wore bandages
wet in vinegar on my wrists, and went
without sleep till I was completely ex-
hausted. The wind was contrary, the
bark could not leave the harbor. The
children complained most bitterly! The
captain sent repeatedly to the town to get a
steamship to tow his vessel out, but ob-
tained nothing but promises. At length
Mr. Tompkins went to engage a steamer.
One came, but he was not with it. The
captain said he had come to take twenty
passengers off the bark. Mrs. Tompkins
inquired why her husband was not with
the steamer: The reply was, “He has
been left by mistake.” She gave credence
to the assertion and hurried us to remove
our goods immediately. My brother-in-
law would not comply, believing it a de-
ception. Eagerly seventeen rushed on to
the steamer, so frantic were we to escape fiom
the furious insects.
	We floated on with speed, found the boat
comfortable. Mrs. Tompkins was not at
all troubled about her husband. She was
certain he knew the way to San Francisco.
The first event on the boat was a long dis-
cussion with a gentleman on the doctrines
of the Mormon church. I preached to him
a Gospel sermon, bore my testimony to a
new dispensation, a renewal of all the gifts
and blessings. He seemed greatly sur-
prised. I left for his perusal a pamphlet
by Orson Pratt.
	We went to our staterooms with high
hopes of rest, but found we had only made
an exchange in the nature of our foes: the
latter having their habitation in the berths.
We struggled through the night, the morn-

[Column 3]
ing found us in the harbor. We made our
way to our friends’ homes, they received us
with great cordiality and made us welcome.
Mrs. Tompkins settled with the captain.
Four days after, Mr. Tompkins arrived. He
was highly offended because we had left
the bark in his absence. He assured us he
was there in four hours after we left with a
steamer to tow the boat into the bay, where
she could make her way alone. His wife’s
feelings were injured by the severity of his
remarks, and he was forced to make an
apology.
	The hearts of the brethren and sisters
who had landed there in ’46 from the old
ship Brooklyn, were open to receive us, to
supply our wants and prepare us for our
voyage across the seas. I found a Sister
Corwin, mother of Samuel Brannan’s wife,
an exceedingly kind-hearted woman, full of
faith and good works. She lived with her
daughter in a house like a king’s palace.
With Sister William Morey I felt much at
home. Shared largely of her benevolence. 
Brother John Lewis came with his carriage
and took us to Mission Dolores, three miles
from San Francisco. The old mission
house was built the year of  “Independence.’’
It resembles some old, ancient abbey,
which it is, built by the Catholics and
used for a nunnery. ’Tis of immense size,
long, dark alleys; with awe they strike the
mind. We spent two weeks there, were
kindly entertained. Numbers there were
in that branch of the Church who showed
us great kindness. A better class of citi-
zens never lived in San Francisco, a city
for money and business. A large fleet of
ships were lying in harbor; the sailors had
all deserted and gone to the mines; no
hands left to the management of the vessels.
They looked like a funeral procession.
There had been a terrible fire in the city a
few months before we arrived, but rapidly
have they made repairs.
	Ten days after Mr. Tompkins came in,
the bark arrived with the remainder of the
passengers. She had been baffling about
two weeks in the bay. Those of us who
had left her felt ourselves fortunate; but
when Mr. Tompkins complained that his
expenses had been too great, I felt it sorely!
Greatly did I regret that I had not means
of my own to go on an errand for the Lord.
What I do for the cause of Christ, I desire
to do at my own expense: and I pray for
means, that I may show my liberality and
willingness to do what is essential to be
done. Mrs. Tompkins never but once re-
ferred to what her husband had done for
my family, and then it was in a passion,
and I forgave her. She is good and true,
and her faith in the latter-day work is
strong.
	After a tarry of five weeks in San Fran-
cisco our passage was engaged to Tupuai,
on board the brig Jane A. Hersey, Capt.
Salmon. We set sail the 15th of Septem-
ber, in fulfillment of a dream my eldest
daughter had two years before, being the
precise time shown in the dream. Myself
and four daughters, my sister, her husband
and one son, two other families, six Elders,
in all twenty souls embarked.
	We found the captain a polite, well-bred gentleman; likewise Capt. Hall, who be-
longed on Tahiti, was returning as passen-
ger, a kind and agreeable man. Mr. Poole,
part owner in the vessel, was on board with
his wife, a sprightly young English lady of
seventeen, very intelligent and agreeable.
There were elements of good society, and
but for the intolerable seasickness we might


have had a delightful voyage. The com-
mon sailors were extremely obliging, striv-
ing to do little acts of kindness to attract
our attention. With the poor fellows a
kind word is appreciated. Our cabin was
large, built on purpose for our accommo-
dation. I had never supposed I should be
sick on the water, but on the contrary I was
the first one taken. I could not endure
the confined air in the cabin. In the morn-
ing my bed was made on deck for the day.
Not a mouthful could I eat except when
lying down. It seemed like raising the
dead to get out of my berth and dress my-
self, yet when I found my life depended
upon it, I could accomplish it by a power-
ful exertion. No dread to me was like that
of being buried in the sea. At times when
I felt like I could close my eyes and drop
out of existence, the thought that they
would bury me beneath the billowing
waves that dashed and foamed against the
bulwarks of the grand old brig, would in
an instant arouse me to a desperate exertion
to go on deck. And well did it happen for
me; my two eldest daughters are natural
sailors, only for a day or two did they com-
plain at all, could walk the deck when the
ship was reeling to and fro. Ellen was at-
tentive and faithful during the whole voy-
age of thirty-five days. Day after day I
lay on the deck, my bones aching, on my
hard bed, bewailing my sad fate, that I
must be forever traveling over the world
without my companion. Sometimes I was
able to read, which was a solace and com-
fort. My secret prayer was to be preserved
from a watery grave.
	When the cry of porpoise or skipjack was
heard, I would call for some one to lift me
up and let me cling to the bulwarks, that I
might feast my eyes on the wonders of the
mighty deep. At such times I would near-
ly forget my weakness. I wished continu-
ally for excitement. Seasickness, how hor-
rible! always dying, and yet you live! The
whole company were sick, no one felt like
praying and administering to his fellows. I
longed to hear prayers, and when at length
the Elders resolved to perform that duty,
and lay on hands to rebuke the disease, we
felt relieved and our hearts were comforted.
I had been severely tried because the
Elders did not officiate in religious duties
on board the vessel. They feared an ex-
citement, knowing the officers were infidel
in their views. The apology did not satis-
fy me. I kept silence, even from good
words, but it was pain and grief to me.
The captain knew we were missionaries.
There were some persons among us who
were disposed to carry tales from one to
another, a principle so detestable that any
lover of peace should spurn it as he would
a venomous serpent.
[To be continued.]


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


(Column 1)
R. S. REPORTS.
		RIGBY WARD.

	Minutes of the Rigby ward Relief Soci-
ety conference held April 27, 1899.
	The meeting was presided over by
Counselor Eleanor Homer. Commenced
by singing, “O, give me back my prophet
dear.” Prayer by Sister Fanny Cellers.
Continued by singing “We thank Thee,
O God, for a prophet.” Roll called and
minutes of the previous meeting read and
accepted.
	Sister Fanny Cellers said she was thank-
ful to meet with the Saints, felt the Spirit
of God was with us, gave good counsel and

(Column 2)
spoke comforting words to those who were
in trouble. Said it was the duty of every
member to sustain those who were placed
over them: not only by donations, but by
being present at meeting and attending our
duties, that we might always be found
faithful and be united as a band of sisters,
ever striving to go onward and upward,
that when we were through with this life
we could say, ‘‘I have fought the fight and
finished the work Thou hast set me to do.”
	Sister L. J. Barrows asked God to give
her strength to fight against all weaknesses
that flesh is heir to. Made some excellent
remarks on the training of children, and
spoke of the benefits our children would
receive if we would always set them a good
example; for by so doing we were laying
up treasures in heaven, “where neither
moth nor rust could corrupt.”
	Sister Allred related some of her past ex-
periences, and spoke comforting words to
our beloved president, who was bowed
down with grief through the death of her
lovely babe.
	President S. A. Cordon said if it were
not for her faith and hope in the Gospel her
troubles would be more than she could
bear. She knew if she were worthy she
would receive her child again, and that
was a great comfort to her.
	Sister Fanny Cellers sweetly and patheti-
cally sang “Afflicted Saints.”
	Sister Eleanor Homer offered up a prayer
for the special benefit of the bereaved sis-
ter. She afterwards gave some good instruc-
tions; suggested that the Relief Society set
two sisters apart to visit the Primary Asso-
ciation every two weeks. 
	S. F. Saxton bore her testimony, and de-
sired to be ever ready to help to roll the
work along.
	Meeting was adjourned for two weeks.
Singing, “The Spirit of God like a fire is
burning.” Benediction by Jerusha Allred.
				ELEANOR HOMER.

		BEAR LAKE STAKE.
	Minutes of the conference of the Relief
Society of Bear Lake Stake, held in the
Paris Tabernacle, Sunday June 11th, 1899.
	Meeting was called to order at 9:30 a. m.
by President Julia Lindsay. The choir
and congregation sang, “Our God we raise
to Thee.” Prayer by President William
Budge. Singing, “Softly beams the sacred
dawning.” Roll called: Stake officers
present, six; Society officers, ten.
	President Lindsay said that words failed
to express the gratitude of her heart on this
occasion when we are permitted to meet
under such favorable circumstances and
have with us Apostle Franklin D. Richards,
Sisters Jane S. Richards and F. S. Rich-
ards.
	Apostle Richards said: “The sisters
have requested me to occupy the time this
morning, which I will do cheerfully. I
shall have to leave your meeting before it
closes, in order to fill an appointment in
Salt Lake City tomorrow. My feelings
have been touched by the kindness which
has been shown us since we have been
here, and I rejoice greatly in mingling with
you. This glorious work was commenced
by the boy Joseph in 1820; he accomplished
a great deal from then until 1844. The
Saints will go to Jackson Co., when certain
things have been accomplished. When
the proper time comes, the Lord will open
the way before us.”
	The speaker then dwelt at length upon

(Column 3)
the law of tithing. Said the Saints were
plainly shown the necessity of its observ-
ance, and the blessings to be enjoyed by
those who are honest with the Lord. He
had seen its beginning, and had endeavored
to observe it from that day until the present,
and knew there was great satisfaction in so
doing. Everything we possess should be
tithed. Invoked heaven’s blessings upon
the Saints.
	Sister F. S. Richards had very much de-
sired that in Apostle Richards’ remarks he
would direct a few words to the sisters.
Said: “Though there are many of the
brethren who are willing to aid us, there
are none more willing to labor with and
encourage us than is he.” Spoke of the
great influence our sisters have among
ladies who do not belong to our Church.
We mothers and daughters should care
more for our bodies; we should have a
proper understanding of physical laws.
We should understand the training of chil-
dren, the most important work we have.
Emphasized the great necessity of young
ladies attending Relief Society meetings,
and thus be benefited by the experience of
our older sisters, and partake of the holy
influence we feel in mingling together.
	President Wm. Budge was exceedingly
pleased to hear the law of tithing discoursed
upon by Apostle Richards; the Saints need
awakening to its importance. One tenth
of all we possess belongs to the Lord, and
if we keep it we are robbing Him. Now
is a time for a more full development of
our spiritual nature. "May God bless and
help us to carry out that which we
know to be true; which I ask in Jesus
name."
	The choir sang "The song of the re-
deemed.” Benediction by President James
H. Hart.
	Afternoon session was called to order at
1:40 p. m. by President Lindsay. After
the usual devotional exercises the verbal
reports were given as follows:
	Paris 1st ward, President Jane Stucki;
Paris 2nd ward, President Ashley; Ovid,
President Jensen; Lanark, President Par-
ker; Liberty,Counselor Nora Austin.
	President Lindsay called for a report
from North Liberty; stated that the society
having just been organized there would be
nothing particular to report.
	Bern was reported by President Kunz;
Bennington, President Vanorman; Mont-
pelier, President Hoganson; Wardboro, 
Counselor Kutch; Dingle, President Sparks;
Thomas Fork, President Dalton; Bloom-
ington, President Patterson; St. Charles,
Secretary S. Young; Fish Haven, Counselor
Stock; six societies not reported.
	President Lindsay said it would be folly
for her to occupy the time when we have
with us the sisters who have come from a
distance to visit us, as we are all desirous
of hearing from them. “You are all aware 
of the death of our beloved sister and my
counselor, Elizabeth Collings, with whom
I labored without a jar for years. It is
necessary for me to have another counselor 
to assist me.”
	It was then moved, seconded and carried
that Sister L. M. Hart act as first counselor
in place of Sister Collings; also that Sister
Mary Bagley act as second counselor.
	Sister Jane S. Richards complimented
the sisters on their interesting and brief re-
ports; they were given in a businesslike
manner. We shall have much joy and
satisfaction in our old age if we are always 
kind to one another, and in every way pos-


sible try to lighten each others’ burdens.
Our prayers should be sincere, they should
come from the heart; they should not be
formal, but we should ask for that which
we really desire, as we would ask an earth-
ly parent. Related some of the trying
scenes through which she had passed in the
early settlement of Salt Lake valley, and
how the Lord had provided for her in times
of need. Exhorted us all to be kind to our
fellow beings, feed the hungry, visit and
comfort the afflicted, and not utter a
word that would wound the feelings of
another. Did not advocate programs as a
rule in our Relief Society meetings.
The speaker’s remarks throughout were
excellent, and no doubt will be the means
of many living purer and more charitable
lives.
Sister F. S. Richards said we should en-
courage one another, both our boys and our
girls. Do not wait until people are dead
before we speak well of them. Sister
Richards read a poem composed by Sister
Eliza R. Snow, “The Object of Relief
Societies.” Spoke at some length in an
intelligent manner upon the necessity of
being kind to the poor whoever they may
be.
President Lindsay hoped we might have
sufficient of the good spirit to enable us to
practice the good teachings we have heard
during our conference. Felt very thank-
ful to our sisters for their visit, to the choir
and all who have taken an interest in our
conference.
President James H. Hart expressed great
pleasure in having attended the conference.
We have enjoyed the influence of the
Holy Spirit.
The choir sang an anthem. Benediction
by Bishop Lewis.
N. H. ALLRED, Sec.

SALT LAKE STAKE.
The quarterly conference of the Salt
Lake Stake Relief Society convened in the
Assembly Hall, Saturday, June 3, 1899, at
10 a. m. Present on the stand were Presi-
dent M. I. Horne and Counselor Clara C.
Cannon, President Zina D. H. Young,
Sisters Bathsheba W. Smith, Maria Wilcox
and Maria Nebeker. 
Meeting was called to order by President 
M. I. Horne. Singing, “Arise, O glorious
Zion.” Prayer by Sister Maria Nebeker.
Singing, “O happy home! O blest abode!”
Roll called: twenty city wards and sixteen
country wards responding. Minutes of the
morning session of the previous conference
read and approved.
President M. I. Horne was sorry to see
so few present. It was required of us by
the authorities to meet and transact busi-
ness and instruct each other, and if we did
not do so we would be the losers. The
officers of the society should make an effort
to be present. The Lord will not excuse
us for carelessness and indifference. If we
as mothers set an example of faithfulness,
our children will be apt to follow that ex-
ample. Spoke briefly on the law of tith-
ing. Hoped the sisters would be faithful
to all the duties imposed upon them,
whether of a public or private, character.
Proposed that Sister Clarissa S. Williams
be sustained as assistant recording secre-
tary, which was unanimously carried.
Sister Horne then called for reports from
twenty-one of the city branches. Very in-
teresting and instructive reports were given
by the presidents or other representatives,

[Column 2]
showing an increase in membership and
general interest. The branches are in a
very prosperous condition considering the
financial depression in the city. All were
very much interested in the work, and
were anxious to do their duty to the fullest
extent.
Sister Horne made a few brief remarks of
encouragement, and expressed her satis-
faction in the reports given.
Meeting then adjourned until 2. p. m.
Singing, “Lord dismiss, us with Thy'bless-
ing.” Benediction by Sister Maria Wilcox.
Afternoon session: Meeting called to
order by President M. I. Horne. Singing,
“Come, come, ye Saints.” Prayer by
Sister Webb. Singing, “Now let us re-
joice.” Minutes of afternoon session of
last conference read and approved.
President Zina D. H. Young was the
first speaker. Said we have left all for the
Gospel’s sake, and we should be willing to
make personal sacrifices for the same. We
need to meet often and partake of the
beautiful spirit attending the meetings of
the Saints. Gave some very timely in-
structions to mothers, telling them they
should be strictly honest and truthful in all
their actions, as children are very quick to
notice and know what mothers do. Mother-
hood is a grand privilege, and we should
honor that sacred trust. Was thankful
that the days of using the rod were past;
it was so beautiful to rule by love. Said
that she had been in the Church sixty-
eight years, and had enjoyed her life in
trying to live up to the glorious principles
of the Gospel. We want to live so that we
can have the spirit of the Lord with us al-
ways.
Sister Bathsheba W. Smith said she de-
sired to be directed by the spirit of the
Lord in every action in life. Believed in
being true to her religion and her friends.
Thought if we loved our religion we
would be truthful and honest, prayerful
and upright. Spoke briefly on the law of
tithing. Said we should live within our
means, that now is the time to learn to
love each other. God is love, and if we
would be prospered and blessed, we must
lead and not drive. Believed that inas-
much as we strive for the blessings of the
Lord, He will bless us.
Professor C. J. Thomas, Sister Maggie
S. Hull and, Sister Maria B. Winder sang
“I know that my Redeemer lives.”
President M. Isabella Horne was the
next speaker. She said that good singing
was a very great source of blessing to us
and those who possess good voices should
feel very grateful. Urged the sisters to
attend their meetings, and spoke of the
good accomplished by attending the semi-
monthly meetings. Said our faith and
works go together, and we will be judged
by them, no matter what our professions.
Spoke of the law of tithing, quoting from
the third chapter of Malachi, and referred
the sisters to the revelation in the Doctrine
and Covenants for instruction on the sub-
ject. Spoke on the subject of storing
wheat: said if the societies did not own
granaries they should store flour in cans.
Sister Sarah Jane Cannon gave an inter-
esting account of a visit in company with
Sister Emma Woodruff, to the Sevier Stake
Relief Society. They found the sisters
energetic and fully alive to their duties.
Sister Clara C. Cannon advised the sis-
ters to read the revelations of the Lord, and
be posted as to the requirements made of

[Column 3]
us as Latter-day Saints. Spoke impres-
sively of the fact that President Snow had
received a revelation in regard to the law of
tithing. Said we should teach our chil-
dren this principle it is a blessing for us to
obey it; it is to test us, bless us and
strengthen us. If we obey it, we are the
gainers.
Sister Pixton, of Taylorsville, was hap-
py to be present. Said we had had good
instructions, why do we not obey them and
put them in practice? “Let us do so; let us
keep out of debt, do right and serve the
Lord with all our might, mind and strength.
Be honest in paying tithing and in every
duty in life.” She thanked the Lord for
every blessing she enjoyed. Said we
should teach our children to honor their
parents, so that their days may be long.
Sister Mary Freeze bore testimony of the
truth of the Gospel, and said she knew for
herself that the Lord had spoken to Presi-
dent Snow concerning the law of tithing.
Hoped to be one of those worthy to help
build up Jackson County.
Sister Mary Hyde was glad to be present,
was always glad to hear the name of the
Prophet Joseph Smith mentioned. She 
knew he was a true prophet of God, and re-
joiced that we have prophets now to speak
to us and tell us the will of the Lord.
By request Sister Maggie S. Hull sang
“The Beautiful City.”
Sister Clarissa S. Williams was glad to
be present. Thought the instructions re-
ceived from the sisters of great benefit if
we would only seek to carry them out.
Thought the labors of the Relief Society a
great and noble work.
A vote of thanks was heartily given
Brother Thomas and Sisters Hull and
Winder for the beautiful music they had
furnished during the meeting.
The Relief Society presidents were re-
quested to see that reports were sent to
Secretary Augusta W. Grant, No. 14 South,
2nd East St., by the 15th of July. Meeting
was then adjourned to meet the first Satur-
day in September.
After singing the Doxology, President
Horne dismissed the meeting in tongues.
The interpretation was given by Sister 
Paul.

AUGUSTA W. GRANT, Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


OBITUARY.
	MYRA CLAYTON WINTER.
	It is with feelings of sorrow that we record the
death of Sister Myra Clayton Winter, which oc-
curred at Georgetown, Idaho, June 22, 1899.
	Deceased was born in Mansfield, Nottingham-
shire, England, November 3, 1822. She em-
braced the Gospel at an early age, and emigrated
to Salt Lake City with her sister Sarah, in the fall
of 1853. In July of the following year she be-
came the wife of Thomas W. Winter, then Bishop
of the 5th ward. She became the president of
the first Primary Association organized in that ward,
which position she faithfully filled until her duties
as a nurse compelled her to resign.
	Since the death of her husband in 1882, she has
spent most of her time with her children in Rex-
burg and Georgetown, Idaho.
	Sister Winter was a very kind and loving
mother and a true friend, and her death will be
mourned by all who knew her. She was the
mother of six children, four of whom are still liv-
ing: Bishop T. J. Winter and Mrs. Sarah Rands,
of Rexburg, and Harriet Smith and Celena
Leveridge, of Georgetown.
	Sister Winter was buried, according to her re-
quest, at the place of her death. She died as she 
had lived, in full faith of the Gospel, and in the
hope of a glorious resurrection.
	COM.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		LET US HAVE PEACE

	(An address delivered by Mrs. Josie E.
Childs at the recent peace meeting at Orange-
ville.)

	Being chosen to address this large assem-
bly on a subject of vital importance to us
all, I respond with hesitancy, fearing my
inability to handle this great subject with
the intelligence that is due.
	If we look upon the world today as one
great Brotherhood, our hearts will readily
go out to all for their peace and happiness.
And I hope for the success of every nation.
	There is in the heart of every soul a love
for his native land. No melody is so sweet
to him as a national air; no music that can
stir the inner heart with greater depths; no
song that makes the wanderer’s thought
revert homeward as effectually as a song of
his own dear native land! And this is
right, and that right should be respected by
all, except when it infringes upon the
rights of others; then it ceases to be a right.
	It is a hereditary tradition matured in the
breast of man, to support and sustain his
motherland. It is a natural thing for in-
dividuals, neighbors, communities, states
or nations to differ in opinions; to aspire to
greatness above their fellows; to become
jealous of the capabilities that are apparent,
and contention, discord and strife are en-
gendered within their hearts, and enmity is
the result. When this state of affairs is
reached, it only requires a little thing to
fan into flame the burning fire of action.
And that action means, when implied to
nations, war. Nothing brings to the
heart of a patriot the spirit of revenge
quicker than an insult to his country, and
that revenge calls for bloodshed. This is
the great effect of the traditional education
that has been matured in the hearts of men
since time immemorial.
	Today not only our beloved nation, but
the entire world is in a great commotion.
War and rumor of war is extant all over
the surface of the earth; and in the words
of the great prophet: "Peace has been
taken from the earth."
	Fear has taken the place of content.
Men are called from their vocations of life
to fill the ranks in the great battles that are
pending. No more you hear from the
shop of the smith the clang of the ham-
mer in his great brawny hands on the 
heated steel at the forge, accompanied by
his mirthful song. No more the happy
farmer sings all care away, as he gently
turns the soil in preparation for the seed,
and dreams a dream of contentment in the
anticipation of a bounteous harvest of 
"golden grain," weaving the while a
pleasant thought of his loved wife and
little ones, and their wants being abundant-
ly supplied. No more the wife sings
about her work preparing the frugal meal
for her dear ones. All is changed!
	The smith's hammer lies where it fell,
when he hastened to his country's call. The 
shop is empty, he has gone. The husband,
father and son, have gone to defend their
country's honor. The song of gladness is
hushed on the lips of the once happy wife
and mother. A look of anguish and des-
pair has settled upon her face, as her gaze
follows the retreating forms of her dear
ones, whom she may never see again. And,
with her little ones, kneeling before the
Great Father who will never desert her,
she petitions Him for the safety of her
loved ones, her husband, her sons, and in

[Column 2]
her heart of hearts, sues and pleads for
peace.
	The misery and distress of war are terrible!
Want follows in its wake, and today thou-
sands are suffering the pangs of hunger—
literally starving for food that cannot be
had. Many hearts are torn with anguish
at the death of their loved ones! Many
homes are blighted; and even those fortun-
ate enough to return, through fatigue and
exposure are maimed perhaps to such an
extent they will never be able to go back to
their old occupations. And perhaps, through
the life-long suffering before them, they
will regret that they were permitted to live
at all!
	Oh, my friends, this is not a picture
drawn from the imagination, that will per-
ish through the lapse of days! There are
many poor men and boys, living and dead,
that stand as a monument of the distressing
reality of war!
	Looking at the world as it stands today,
should we not with one accord plead for
peace? Should we not seek a better way
to adjust our wrongs? Should we not
petition the law-makers of every country
to devise means that wrongs may be righted
without war? Should we not pray the
Great Ruler that He will touch the
hearts of the law-makers of the earth, and
reveal to them a more perfect system of
righting wrongs, that peace may be re-
stored to the earth again? May our efforts
accrue to bettering the conditions that exist!
	Christ said: “A good man out of the
good treasures of his heart brings forth
that which is good.” May we hope that
we all are sufficiently good that a blessing
for good may follow this move; that peace
may be restored; that the earth will yield in
its strength; that want and misery may
be banished, and prosperity take its place.


AMERICAN WOMEN TAKE TEA
		WITH THE QUEEN.
	A number of the American, colonial and
continental guests of the International Con-
gress of Women having expressed desires
to pay their respects to the queen, Lady
Aberdeen arranged a trip to Windsor on
the afternoon of July 7th, when her majesty
assented to the suggestions that she should
drive slowly through the quadrangle of the
castle and receive a few of the more prom-
inent delegates. It was about twenty
minutes past five when the delegates took
up a position in front of the private en-
trance. A quarter of an hour later her
majesty appeared and Lady Aberdeen made
the presentations.
	Miss Susan B. Anthony, who, with Mrs.
May Wright Sewall, the newly elected
president of the congress, enjoyed the priv-
ilege of presentation, said to the cor-
respondent of the Associated Press after-
wards: ‘‘I had never seen the queen be-
fore, and could not but feel a thrill when,
looking into her wonderful face, I saw her,
as her life is going out, welcoming this
woman’s movement which is the precursor
of the twentieth century. What pleased
me most was when her majesty said: ‘Now,
cannot have the ladies who are visiting
me return without giving them a cup of
tea.’
	“Sir Arthur Briggs, the queen’s private
secretary, replied: ‘But, your majesty, they
are here in hundreds. ’
	“ ‘I don’t care,’ said the queen, ‘if they
are in thousands, they must all have a cup


of tea when they come to see me.’ And
we had it in the queen’s palace as a recog-
nition of the great womanhood of our
country." 
	Mrs. Sewall said: “The queen looked
ten years younger than when I saw her 
three years ago. The whole bearing of
her majesty was a symbol that she, who
has governed the mightiest of empires for
sixty-three years, has finally recognized
intuitively that she crowns her work in
recognizing the claims of her sex to equal
rights with men.”
