O GOD, THE DARKNESS ROLL AWAY.

	(Sung at Women’s Meeting on International
arbitration, London,  June 27, by Miss Esther
Palliser.)

O God ! the darkness roll away 
		Which clouds the human soul,
	And let thy bright and holy day
		Speed onward to its goal !

	Let every hateful passion die
		Which makes our brethren foes,
	And war no longer raise its cry
		To mar the world’s repose.

	How long shall glory still be found
		In scenes of cruel strife,
	Where misery walks, a giant crowned,
Crushing the flowers of life?

	O hush, Great God, the sounds of war,
		And make thy children feel 
That he, with Thee, is nobler far
		Who toils for human weal —

	Let faith, and hope, and charity,
		Go forth through all the earth;
	And man in holy friendship be
		True to his heavenly birth.

						WILLIAM GASKELL.


THE EVERLASTING DAY. 
(Sung by Miss Ella Walker at the Women’s
Meeting on  International Arbitration.) 
I stood on a lofty mountain 
And I gazed over land and sea,
And thought of life's wondrous fountain, 
Its source, and Eternity. 
I thought of God's mighty secrets, 
And how His Myst'ries blend, 
The past without beginning, 
A future without end. 
I looked on the boundless splendor 
Till its marvels dimm’d my sight, 
Then slept in a calm amazement, 
And dream'd with a strange delight;
I dreamed of a Heav'nly City 
Where soul with soul will blend,
Where time has no beginning, 
Where bliss will never end. 
The dream was a revelation, 
A vision of vanquish'd strife, 
A sweet imagination 
Of pure and hallow'd life:
A vision of God's great bounty, 
When sin shall have passed away, 
Of souls from sleep awaking 
To everlasting day. 
R. S. WELLEBOURNE.


[Column 1]
A WOMAN MISSIONARY.
	[Mrs. Addison Pratt on the Society Islands
1850.]
		[Continued.]
	Mr. T. Whittaker, an intelligent Eng
lishman, was living on the Island with a
Tahitian wife, a good, kind woman, but
resembling him as little as a bear resembles
a sheep. He removed with her to Tahiti,
where he zealously engaged in preaching
the Gospel. He is an excellent man, full
of faith and good works, and fine looking.
In his own country he might have married
a woman of culture and refinement; but his
lot was cast here, and it seems to be the
custom with white men who come here
from different nations to intermarry with
the people, leaving their wives when they
return to their respective countries, some-
times taking their children home to be edu-
cated.
	To my children it seemed cruel that after
performing such a long and tedious journey
they were not permitted to meet their fath-
er. Towards the last of January Capt.
Johnson’s schooner was descried in the dis-
tance, coming from Tahiti. The pilot went
off to it, and returned with the joyful in-
telligence that “Paraita” was on board.
Great was the excitement! we were afraid
to believe it. King Tomatoa sent for us to
come to the beach and look through his spy-
glass and view the vessel and the pas-
sengers. We did so, and beheld the long
expected man walking the deck. He had
grown very fleshy, looked surprisingly
large. The news flew like lightning over
the village, and the whole population as-
sembled at the landing to greet and wel-
come their old friend and missionary. He
had been three years absent from them, and
great were their declamations of joy on
seeing him return to their Island. Not-
withstanding their eagerness to grasp his
hand, as they crowded to the water’s edge,
they parted to make an opening for his
wife and children to salute him first, show-
ing more consideration than many persons
would in more civilized life. It was a great
day for us all. He brought with him a bushel
of Tahitian oranges, the sweetest in the
world; likewise a quantity of Vii apples of
peculiarly delicious flavor, a fruit we had
never seen before. The natives hastened
to make a feast, and a great one it was, for
the vast amount of food that was cooked.
Brother Busby had left the preceding day
for Tahiti. I had contemplated going on
the same vessel, had I not received an im-
pression that it was not right to go. I
should have passed my husband on the
way, and been left on Tahiti an uncertain
length of time.
	As soon as the accustomed ceremonies
were over, the building of a schooner was
commenced at Mahu, on the opposite side
of the Island. The material was taken
from San Francisco to build the cabin. Mr.
Grouard was competent to construct the
vessel from the keel to the topmast, and
Mr. Pratt could make the sails. The vessel
was to be of eighty tons burthen, with
welve double berths in the cabin, a centre

[Column 2]
table, transom and library. This was for
the benefit of the mission. It was built of 
tamara wood, (Island mahogany) timber of
the most enduring kind. The native men
saw their lumber with pit saws; they make
beautiful chests of their mahogany, also 
bedsteads and lounges of timber resembling
black walnut. The name of this wood is
mikii.
	Every day found me at the old “Fere
bare ra” (prayer house) teaching the native
children, in their own language, to read and
write; my own and the half-breeds in Eng-
lish. The remainder of the time I devoted
to my journal and letter writing, together
with studying and translating the Hawaiian
language. The whole time was entirely
occupied. Mrs. Tompkins and her two
little daughters I taught regularly. Her
education had been sadly neglected in early
life, but she seemed ambitious and deter-
mined to conquer the first rudiments, and
learn to read and write. She has good
natural talents. What a misfortune to be
neglected in childhood! Mr. Tompkins, 
though a little better educated, is not her
superior in judgment. He is very hospit-
able, but requires those who share his liber-
ality to feel their obligations; thus caus-
ing his benevolence to lose half its value.
Time passed on, and we had little to
complain of but fleas and mosquitoes; they
were terribly annoying! They have their
seasons. In winter (July and August) the
fleas are innumerable. In summer (Decem-
ber and January) mosquitoes take the place
by storm. No venomous reptiles. One
only, called the santipee, whose sting is
like that of a bee. Soft mud will extract
the poison. The work on the schooner
still progressed, the white men and natives
all working with a will. Occasionally a
company of us went across the mountains
to visit them at their work. Though dis-
tant five miles it was a delightful walk, the
road nearly the whole way being shaded by
beautiful trees, some bearing delicious fruit,
which we partook of as we sat down to
rest.
	About the middle of May, ’51, we were
all invited over to witness the launching of
the new schooner. Mr. Whittaker had just
completed a new sail boat; in that we set
sail about sundown. The moon was at its
full, and shining in all its loveliness. The
sea was calm, we had a delightful ride on
the water. Our friends were waiting our
arrival, had a blazing fire lightening through
the timbers: a most romantic sight. There
were ten females, old and young, in the
boat. Our native friends came out to meet
us on the reefs, and carried us in their arms
to dry land. The singers had long been
practicing new tunes to sing on the grand
occasion. The vessel had to be drawn a
considerable distance by hand; it would re-
quire all the strength the island afforded.
Those who were not members of the Church
protested they would not lend their aid un-
less the king would grant them a dance.
Permission was given, great preparations
were made and untiring skill exercised
to invent ornaments. They were engaged
in making native cloth from the bark of the


[Column 1]
paper mulberry tree for a long time before
the launching. They made it fine, bleached
it to a snowy whiteness, then stamped
with brilliant colors, all of home produc-
tion. Shapes of birds, flowers and fishes
were painted on their robes, also letters and
wheels, all of the brightest tints, which
contrasted strikingly with the white ground-
work. Both male and female were dressed in
white, their robes all the same fashion, made
whole, two yards in length, with a hole in
the center to put the head through. These
thrown carelessly over their white dresses
gave them a tasteful appearance.
	Early Monday morning we were assem-
bled to witness the parade. Officers were
chosen to conduct the movements. A
messenger was sent before to announce
their approach. There were two large com-
panies, each company occupying its own
space of ground in the dance. They had
no music, except their own vocal elements,
so loud and clear that one might imagine
himself listening to an Italian band. Their
dancing was with form and order; great
exactness in keeping time. To describe
the different exercises, the unheard of ges-
tures, and the scene throughout, would re-
quire the art of a sculptor and painter;
and they would come short of a just deline-
ation. The pile of food far exceeded all the
other wonders. To see provisions enough
to last two hundred and fifty persons a
whole week, all cooked and laid in one
huge pile, bound in bundles so thick with
leaves that not a particle of air could pene-
trate it, no more than it can jars of fruit
hermetrically sealed. Pigs weighing more
than a hundred weight, roasted whole and
tied up in the usual style, thrown on to the
heap, till it looked like a haystack of leaves.
	When the first exercise of dancing was
over, they commenced pulling at the ropes
fastened to the vessel’s sides, to move it to-
wards the coral reef. Many unsuccessful
efforts were made ere it was started at all.
They were obliged to take advantage of low
tides, or stand in deep water. The king’s
son, who was captain, stood in his place
with the scarlet coat on which I had made
him, and a glittering sword in his hand.
It was his prerogative to give the word of
command. Loud and incoherent ejacula-
tions, such as “a, te, a! te, a!” a signal
for them all to pull together. The wives
of the missionaries were idle spectators.
They had not as yet been invited to take
hold of the ropes. At length an old man
fastened on an extra rope and invited them
to help pull. We all stepped forward and
took hold of the rope with a will! It so
happened at that instant the vessel moved
for the first time. Loud acclamations re-
sounded, almost deafening, about the
strength of the “mau vahime papa,” (foreign
women.) For four successive days it was
moved a short distance each day. The
labor continued till the food was all con-
sumed; then the work was suspended to
cook more. We returned to our homes be-
fore the vessel was launched, those who
could not render important service.
	About the middle of May the schooner
Ravai was brought around to the village
where we lived. Great rejoicing was mani-
fest when she was seen under full sail.
She was anchored a mile and a half from
town. We all walked to the place and
went on board; sailed around till we were
opposite the town, and were taken to shore
in the small boat. Preparations had been
made for a voyage to Tahiti and the Chain
Islands. Mr. Grouard was appointed cap-

[Column 2]
tain and Mr. Alexander was first mate.
	Mr. Pratt and our eldest daughter took
passage on board the new ship Bavai. We
stood on the beach and saw her move off so
majestically, and we felt proud of her. The
native women who had sons on board, gone
for their first voyage, commenced weeping
and wringing their hands. We had not
thought of shedding tears till we heard
their cries. It is their custom thus to wail
and lament when a friend dies or goes to
sea. They wring their hands and tear their
hair, and make the most piteous lamenta-
tions. The Elders all left the Island except
Brother A. Hanks. He rem[-]ined to be
company for the families of the missionaries.
I expected to be very lonely, but, on the
contrary, we all enjoyed ourselves remark-
ably well.
			[To be continued.]


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.
	The Ladies’ Semi-Monthly Meeting con-
vened July 22, 1899, in the 14th ward hall,
with Counselor Elizabeth Webb presiding.
	Meeting opened by singing “All is well.”
Prayer by Sister Sarah J. Cannon. Con-
tinued by singing, “Who are these arrayed
in white.” Minutes of previous meeting
read and approved.
	President Horne came in; said she
was necessarily late. She always liked to
be present at the opening, as all feel better
when they are present to take part in the
singing and prayer. Said she believed that
the Lord would help any one who would
make an effort to do her duty. There is no
progression unless we make an effort to do
what is required of us. We only stay here
a short time in this preparatory state, and
we receive blessings for what we ac-
complish. Said our work in the Relief
Society is more to the poor Saints, but we
never refuse to do good wherever we can.
It is our duty to know ourselves and teach
our children to know there is a God, and to
serve Him. Spoke of the simplicity of the
Gospel, and how easily it was understood.
Said she thought we all had cause to be
thankful to God for His goodness to us.
	Sister Emily Stevenson felt to rejoice in
the Gospel. She knew if we kept the com-
mandments of God, we would be saved in
His kingdom.
	Sister Mair said that a great many do
not know that this is the church of God,
but it is our duty to explain it to others.
	Sister Wilcox bore testimony that she
knew this was the truth, and she greatly
rejoiced in it.
	Sister Louie Stevenson said: “We are
sent upon the earth on missions, and it is
our duty to bear testimony to all with
whom we come in contact, if they ask for
such.”
	Sister Butterworth felt thankful that she
was one of the workers in this Church.
She had been a busy bee in the “Hive of
Deseret” for forty-five years. Spoke of the
duties of Latter-day Saints. Said: “We have
got the children of the earth to teach the
Gospel to, ourselves to save, our families to
raise and teach them correct principles, and
it behooves us to be very humble and
prayerful.” Testified that there are blessings
attached to the observance of the law of
tithing.
	The congregation sang, “The Spirit of
God like a fire is burning.”
	Sister Peterson knew that Joseph Smith
was a prophet of God, and she was pleased
to be numbered among the Saints.

[Column 2]
	Sister Sarah Bateman said she did not
wish to go away without bearing her testi-
mony. She always thanked the Lord that
she was permitted to mingle with the
Latter-day Saints and work in the Temple.
	Sister Smelie said she thanked the Lord
every day that she was brought into these
mountains—this home of refuge. Said the
Gospel was everything to her.
	Sister Peterson believed if we were faith-
ful and asked our Heavenly Father for
what we wanted, He would grant us all
that is for our good.
	A young sister in the congregation said
she was thankful she was born and raised a
Latter-day Saint, and that she was a mem-
ber of the Relief Society.
	Another sister said we should teach our
children true principles, and be prayerful,
that we may teach them to be Saints.
	Sister Pixton said if we do not put our
testimonies to a good use, God will not be
pleased. She wondered how many would
be ready if the call should come for us to go
out to meet the Bridegroom. It had been
taught to us to be prepared ever since the
days of Nauvoo. Spoke of the sorrow it is
to see our children marry outsiders and go
astray, as so many are doing at the present
time. Spoke of the necessity of doing the
work for the dead, and not idling our time
away.
	Sister Neal was glad to meet with the
sisters and receive the instructions she did
here. Said she was one of the members
when this society was first organized. Spoke
of the many testimonies God gives to His
daughters as well as His sons.
	Another sister bore testimony of the
truth of the work, and spoke of the duties
of those who wish to do work in the Temple.
	Sister Harriet A. Badger said she rejoiced
to be here and meet with these sisters. Felt
we had a great deal to do in a short time.
Knew we must learn to be kind to each
other and love each other.
	Sister Annie T. Hyde said she wondered
if we realized the value of testimony. She
thought it necessary that we should all be
witnesses to this great work. How thank-
ful the Prophet Joseph felt, and how he
was braced up when he had the three wit-
nesses to support him! Thought it neces-
sary for each to bear a testimony of the
Prophet Joseph to her children.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Bene-
diction by Counselor Elizabeth Webb.
				J. S. WOOLEY, Sec.


“WHEN, in 1857, Lord Salisbury married
his wife, he was plain Robert Cecil, twenty-
seven years old, a second son, with no im-
mediate prospect of succeeding to the Mar-
quisate. She was a daughter of a Judge of
the now extinct Court of Exchequer, Baron
Anderson, and older than her husband, but
a woman of great intellectual gifts as well
as personal attractiveness. The marriage
was distasteful to young Cecil’s father, and
for a long time he had to eke out a scanty
income by writing for newspapers, chiefly
the Saturday Review. The death of his
elder brother in 1865 permitted him to as-
sume the courtesy title of Lord Cranborne,
and that of his father, in 1868, made him
Marquis of Salisbury. The lineal des-
cendant of Queen Elizabeth’s Cecil, Lord
Burleigh, has proved himself worthy of his
distinguished ancestor, and is the foremost
of living British statesmen.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


TO SISTER ELIZABETH STEVENSON.
Blessed are they that mourn for they shall be 
comforted.

	Courage, brave soul, they grief allay!
		Though life seems dark and drear,
	It is not so, to cheer thy way
		The angels hover near.

	We know thou'st drunk the bitter cup,
		Aye, drained it o'er and o'er;
	But thou hast laid thy treasures up,
		Where sorrows are no more.

	And thou art faithful; thou art true;
		As years roll swiftly by,
	E'en this great grief thou wilt not rue,
		Nor pain, nor tear, or sigh.

	And, O, thou art so greatly blessed,
		Great joy should fill thine heart,
	That thou art given through righteousness,
		In His great work a part.

	Yea, even that thy voice is heard
		In Zion's stakes afar,
	Proclaiming by thy deeds and word
		The Christ—our guiding star. 

	And couldst thou ask a greater boon,
		Than—called by love divine—
	To work for those beyond the tomb?
		O, what a lot is thine!

	That God will give thee light and trust
		According to they need—
	Thou knowest all His ways are just—
		Thy sisters can but plead.

	Blessed are they that mourn, He said,
		And blessed are the pure;
	In me they shall be comforted,
		My word is strong and sure.”
					RUTH M. FOX.
July 7, 1899


SEND DOWN THY TRUTH.
	(Sung at the Women's Council Arbitration
meeting, London, June 27.)
	Send down Thy truth, O God!
		Too long the shadows frown,
	Too long the darkened way we’ve trod:
		Thy truth, O Lord, send down!

	Send down Thy love, Thy life,
		Our lesser lives to crown,
	And cleanse them of their hate and strife:
		Thy living love send down!

	Send down Thy peace, O Lord!
		Earth’s bitter voices drown
	In one deep ocean of accord:
		Thy peace, O God, send down!
							E. R. SILL.


THE AGE OF GOLD. 
(Sung at Women’s Arbitration meeting, June 27.)

	The God that to the fathers
		Revealed His holy will
	Has not the world forsaken;
		He’s with the children still.
	They envy not the twilight
		That glimmered on their way;
	Look up and see the dawning
		That broadens into day.

	’Twas but far off, in vision,
		The father’s eye could see 
	The glory of the kingdom- 
		The better time to be,
	Today we see fulfilling
		The dreams they dreamt of old;
	While nearer, ever nearer,
		Rolls on the age of gold!

	With trust in God’s free spirit-
		The ever broadening ray.
	Of truth that shines to guide us
		Along our forward way-
	Let us today be faithful,
		As were the brave of old,
	Till we their work completing,
		Bring in the age of gold! 
					M. I. SAVAGE.


MARRIAGE OF MISS E. W. SEARS. 
	At the residence of the bride’s parents,
Hon. and Mrs. S. W. Sears, Thorndike, 
East Waterloo, on Friday at high noon, (12
M.) Miss Emilie W. Sears was mar-
ried to Mr. John Gillette Roberts, of In-
dianapolis, Indiana. The ceremony was 
performed by the Rev. I. Elmer Goshen. The 
wedding was a very quiet one, only mem-
bers of the family being present and one or 
two friends, visitors from Janesville, Wis-
consin.
	The house was beautifully decorated,
the windows and mantlepiece of the parlor, 
where the ceremony took place, were banked
with ferns and palms, brightened with nas-
turtiums of the most charming varieties;
the library was fragrant with lovely roses 
of all shades of color; the hall was deco-
rated with long-stemmed, old-fashioned
hollyhocks, and the dining room, where 
the wedding breakfast was served immedi-
ately after the marriage, was filled in 
every available place with the daintiest-
colored and sweetest of sweet peas. The 
bride wore a gray “going away gown,” with 
white silk waist, and carried a large bouquet 
of exquisite delicate roses, and a dainty 
ivory fan. 
	The newly wedded pair left Salt Lake 
City early the following morning on a wed-
ding tour to New York, Chicago and a 
number of large cities in the East, and 
were given a brilliant reception at the 
residence of the bridegroom’s parents, in 
Indianapolis, while enroute.
	The bride is well known in Salt Lake 
City, and familiarly to those who visit this 
office, as she has assisted here occasionally 
during the last few years and at times con-
tinuously, in the absence of the editor. 
	Mr. Roberts, the bridegroom, taught 
Mathematics in the High school in this city, 
but left here two years ago to accept a more 
remunerative position in Detroit, Michigan, 
where the young people expect to reside, for 
the present, at least. 
	Mr. Roberts is an electrical engineer, and 
is superintendent of the chemical department 
of the Detroit Switchboard Telephone and
Constructing Company, and is reputed to 
be one of the most expert men in his line of 
business in the country. 
	May every blessing desirable for the at-
tainment of complete happiness be showered 
upon these young people just entering, as 
it were, the portals of a new life; and may 
their union prove a happy and prosperous 
one in all that will bring true success in 
the future, both in this life and that which 
is to come. 
	The EXPONENT extends most sincere 
and heartfelt congratulations to Mr. and 
Mrs. Roberts, and trusts the future may 
be fraught with such rich and happy influ-
ences, that their days may flow on smoothly 
and scarce a pebble disturb the waves of 
the ocean of life, over which they glide 
ever onward.

And love is best, pure love without alloy, 
	Of all the blessings and the mercies given;
It brings the sweetest bliss, the highest joy 
	And gives us faith and hope that lights to 
		Heaven.
Dear “Emmie” may your star of love be bright 
	To guide through every mist and every storm;
May showers to prove thy faith be only light 
	And blessed sunshine after cheer and warm. 
									E. B. W.


JEALOUSY is the greatest enemy to
[-]rotherhood.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
	THE Relief Society Conference of this
Stake of Zion, will convene in the Assem-
bly Hall, Saturday, September 2, 1899,
meetings commencing at 10 a. m. and 2 p.
m. It is confidently expected there will be
a large representation from all branches of
the Stake.
	WEDNESDAY evening, August 30, a
lawn party was given at Mrs. Amelia F.
Young’s residence, by her niece, Miss Hat-
tie Wallace to the Young Ladies Chorus
conducted by Madame Swenson, the leader
and teacher of about forty young ladies.
The grounds were prettily decorated with
flags and bunting, and lighted profusely
with every variety of Chinese lanterns,
which, among the trees and heavy foliage,
gave the place a fairy-like appearance, the
bright young girls in soft, airy summer cos-
tumes, many in white, and a few in bright
colors, flitting about here and there, added
greatly to the beauty of the scene, and with
their merry conversation and the delightful
arrangement of the entire affair, gave one
that charming sense of rest in recreation
that is so helpful to those who grow weary
with daily toil and need some sort of
change. There were a number of young
gentlemen present, by invitation, for of
course the girls must have escorts, and we
noticed one or two, perhaps three, of the
volunteers who recently returned from
Manilla. The tables were spread under
the shade of the trees, with dainty refresh-
ments and decorated with a variety of
lovely flowers and viewing the occupants
from available nooks here and there, the
effect was very pretty. Mrs. Young and
her two sisters, Mrs. Wallace and Mrs.
Brown, and a few other matronly women,
including Mrs. Adams, of Parowan, (whose
daughters were among the chorus) seemed
to enjoy the party as much as the younger
people. The evening was beautiful, the
sky clear and the stars brilliant, and every-
thing seemed propitious to the happy oc-
casion.


REUNION AT SALTAIR.
	A pleasant remembrance in the minds of
the U. W. P. C. is the successful reunion
held at Saltair, Thursday, July 12, 1899.
	The members and friends boarded the
4:15 p. m. train, arriving at the beach in
ample time for a dip in the lake. Those
not caring to venture in the water, set the
banquet table, which fairly groaned under
its weight of tempting viands.
	The absence of our esteemed Honorary
President, Mrs. E. B. Wells, who was in
England, was keenly felt.
	At 8:15 p. m. the club returned home,
extending a vote of thanks to the committee
in charge and many good wishes for the
success of the U. W. P. C. 
			AIMEE C. SCHILLER.


FREEDOM.
	I care not who were vicious back of me,
	No shadow of their sins on me is shed
My will is greater than heredity,
	I am no worm to feed upon the dead.

My face, my form, my gestures and my voice,
	May be reflections from a race that was.
But this I know, and knowing it, rejoice,
	I am myself a part of the Great Cause. 

I am a spirit! Spirit would suffice,
	If rightly used, to set a chained world free.
Am I not stronger than a mortal vice
	That crawls the length of some ancestral tree?
				ELLA WHEELER WILCOX.

SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, AUG 15, & SEP. 1, 1899.


[Column 1]
A SPIRITUAL AWAKENING.
	During the summer the visits made in
the several Stakes of Zion by the First
Presidency and Apostles of the Church
and the sisters who have traveled with
them, must have caused quite a spiritual
awakening among the people. Meetings
where the spirit of the Lord has been
poured out in rich abundance, have been
reported from time to time in the daily
papers; and those abroad in other lands, as
well as those at home in Zion, have taken
note of these proceedings and are not un-
mindful of the results which must necessa-
rily follow such special teaching and preach-
ing of the commandments of the Lord.
Naturally a more strict observance of the
law of tithing, of keeping the Sabbath day
holy, the Word of Wisdom and training of
the youth of Zion in paths of virtue and
holiness will follow, and thereby will
come a blessing upon all the interests of
the Saints upon the earth.
	The work is hastening on; the mission-
aries abroad in the world are busy sowing
good seed wherever opportunities can be
made. Distributing tracts from house to
house is done by both men and women
missionaries; also preaching in the public
parks and elsewhere in the open air.
Young women have left good homes, and
some of them who were receiving liberal
salaries for teaching in schools and academ-
ies, to labor in spreading the Gospel in
foreign lands, not for honor, nor the sake
of any remuneration in this world, but that
they might be instrumental in helping on
the cause of truth, which will eventually
redeem mankind from sin.
	The writer rejoiced while in England in
hearing these true and earnest young wom-
en bearing testimony to the knowledge ob-
tained through the Holy Spirit, which
leads into all truth. Such testimony only
comes through humility, prayer and faith-
fulness, and it brings a peace that passeth
understanding.
	Mrs. Lettie Dewey Campbell, Miss Inez
Knight, Miss Zina Chipman, Miss Joseph-
ine Booth, Miss Clara Holbrook, these
young sisters, and also Mrs. Lydia D.
Alder, are all engaged as missionaries, as
laborers in the field, devoting themselves
wholly and solely to the spread of righteous- 
ness and correct principles, giving freely of
that which is more precious than the wealth
of the world.
	The Elders, who without purse or scrip
are proclaiming the everlasting Gospel in

[Column 2]
all parts of the earth, even upon the islands
of the sea, and these with the hundreds of
Home missionaries throughout the several
Stakes of Zion, in the Rocky mountain
region, must certainly arouse greater zeal
and enthusiasm, and make an impression
that cannot be erased on the minds and
hearts of the honest in heart wherever their
lot may be cast. Although there are many
people inclined to scepticism, yet there are
still others who are looking for something
higher and more satisfying than they can
find among the theories heretofore extant
in the world; and occasionally these honest
seekers for truth are found, one here and
another there, and are convinced of divine
revelation to the people in this dispensation
through a prophet sent of God as in days of
old. “Blessed are they who know the
voice of the Good Shepherd,” and are will-
ing to leave all and follow Him.
	The persecution that has been aroused in
our own country, “the land of the free and
the home of the brave,” is most lamentable,
and one cannot but recall the words of the
Savior in contemplating the situation: “It
must needs be that offences come, but woe
be to him by whom they come.” But his-
tory has proven repeatedly that “the blood
of the martyrs is the seed of the Church.”
And there is no doubt of the truth of the
scripture, “Surely the wrath of man shall
praise thee; the remainder of wrath shalt
thou restrain.”


[Column 1]
HOME AGAIN. 
	After a rather prolonged trip abroad for a
busy woman, here we are at the desk again,
everything just as if we had only been
absent a few hours. It is very pleasant to
return home and find one’s family all in
good health, and, in the case of the writer,
to find an addition of a fine pair of twin
boys to the numerous grandchildren, was a
very happy surprise on reaching New York.
A marriage in the family, too, was one of
the surprises to greet our arrival, and alto-
gether only joyful news and occurrences to
greet one and cheer the home-coming.
	To recount the happenings, and even the
principal events of a journey of nearly
three months, is a puzzle; but to one attend-
ing such meetings as the International
Council and Congress of Women in Lon-
don, the official receptions and parties, and
trips here and there, it is utterly impossible
to satisfy one’s self in the least degree.
However, to be able to report good health
and no accidents by land or sea, is in itself
something to rejoice over and to be exceed-
ingly thankful for.
	There were a number of Utah women in
England, all of whom had reached there at
an earlier date than the writer. Some of
them had gone to labor as missionaries,
some on business or pleasure, and others to
attend the International Council and Con-
gress. These were Mrs. Susa Young
Gates, Mrs. Lucy B. Young, Mrs. Eliza-
beth C. McCune, Mrs. M, A. Caine, Mrs.
C. S. Thomas, Miss C. Holbrook and Mrs. L.
D. Alder; the last named has also remained
to do missionary work. The entire journey,
from leaving Salt Lake until the return
home, has been full of interesting occasions
and rich in all that helps to make one’s
time pass happily and “fly, as it were, on
the wings of the wind.”
	At the opening of the Quinquennial on
the afternoon of Monday, June 26, it was
found that the Large Hall, Westminster
Town Hall, would not hold those entitled

[Column 2]
to be present, therefore the crowd was in-
structed to wend its way to Convocation
Hall, Church House, Deanery, Westmins-
ter Abbey, and the International Council
was formally opened in that historic build-
ing  by the Countess of Aberdeen with an
address of welcome, which we hope to pub-
lish in full at some future time. It was a
very able paper, and seemingly touched
upon every subject pertaining to the work
of the Council in the past and suggestions
for future advancement.
	After the address came the introduction
of International officers and the Presidents
of Federated National Councils, which in-
cluded many foreign countries: The United
States, by Mrs. Fannie Humphreys Gaff-
ney, of New York; Canada, Mrs. Boomer;
Germany, Frau Anna Simson; Sweden,
Fru Hierta Retzius; Great Britain and Ire-
land, The Lady Battersea; New South
Wales, The Viscountess Hampden; Den-
mark, Froken Henrie Forschammer; Hol-
land, Madame Klerck von Hogendorp;
New Zealand, Mrs. Pember Reeves.
	Other countries who have signified the
intention of federating with the Inter-
national Council were represented by fra-
ternal delegates, but had no vote. The
only voters (proper) were the officers of the
International Council and Presidents of
National Councils already federated; not
even delegates duly elected had votes.
The countries thus represented were France,
Switzerland, Belgium, Italy, Russia, Aus-
tria, Norway, Victoria, South Australia,
West Australia, Queensland, Cape Colony,
Tasmania, India, Persia, Argentine Re-
public of South America, China and Pales-
tine. Delegates from societies internation-
ally organized (present, but without a vote)
Bureau International Permanent de la Paix;
Federation Abolitioniste Ginirole; Union
Internationale des Ames des Jennes Filles;
World’s Women’s Christian Temperance
Union; World’s Young Women’s Christian
Association; International Union of Press
Clubs; International Order of King’s
Daughters.
	Each person introduced had about five
minutes in which to speak for the country
and Council she represented. This occu-
pied the entire meeting of the afternoon.
The day was very hot and the hall was
crowded. Many distinguished people were
present, both in the seats below and the
front seats in the galleries above. Madame
Von Finklestein Mountford represented the
country of Palestine, where there are as yet
really no organizations of women, but
where it is hoped such like institutions may
soon be established, and also in India,
China and Persia.
	The official reception of the Duchess of
Sutherland at Stafford House was held the
same evening, Monday, June 26, from 9 p.
m. to 12, and was certainly in every par-
ticular a royal entertainment. The house
is one of the richest in architecture and in
all its appointments of finishings and furn-
ishings, which are indescribable. The en-
trance hall is famous throughout England
and is of solid white marble with marble
staircases on either side that cross the upper
hall and are finished with elaborate brass
balustrades. Seated on the wide staircase
at the top of the front opening to the gal-
lery on either side, were four of the most
beautiful women of rank and title. First,
the Countess of Aberdeen; next her, the
Duchess of Sutherland, and farther on to
the right, the Countess of Warwick and her
sister. All these grand ladies we had the


[Column 1]
opportunity of meeting and of being wel-
comed by them most cordially as members 
of the Council.
	Afterwards we passed up the grand stair-
way to the drawing room and other apart-
ments opened for the reception, and passed
where the Hungarian band were discours- 
ing the most exquisite music one could
imagine, which floated thro’ these elegant 
halls, diffusing melody such as is seldom
heard except by the most highly favored of 
the world.
	There were a small number of ladies
whom we had met before, Miss Anthony,
who is always lovely and gracious to her
friends, and Mrs. Sewall, Dr. Shaw, Mrs.
Bedford Fenwick and Mrs. Fenwick Miller
(these two ladies we had been acquainted
with at the Woman’s Congress in Chicago)
and quite a number of others. Mrs. Char-
lotte Perkins Stetson, the grandniece of
Henry Ward Beecher, whom we had met
in Des Moines, Iowa, and who is certainly
one of the brightest women of this nine-
teenth century; who is always sweet and
charming, and who really seemed to take
extra pains for some of our Utah party.
Recently her book on the Economic ques-
tion is attracting world-wide attention, al-
though by many severely criticised. Mrs.
Stetson is a forcible speaker, and also a
brilliant and intellectual writer. She in-
tends visiting Salt Lake City in the near 
future.
	There is not time or space to describe
Stafford House or the wonderful paintings
and banquet, but some time we may per-
haps make separate articles of these great
palaces we have seen and where we have
been entertained.
	Lady Battersea’s reception at Surrey
House was in many respects similar to that
of Stafford House; but at Surrey we were
received by Lord and Lady Battersea, and
it is not the custom, as in America, to be
introduced and passed down a long line of
distinguished people.
	Perhaps in giving the public gatherings 
and entertainments tendered to the Council,
we ought to have mentioned first of all the
informal reception given by the Countess of
Aberdeen on Saturday afternoon, June 24,
previous to the opening of the Council.
This was a very pleasant way of having
the delegates and speakers meet each other,
and a very graceful thing on her part, to
bring the ladies into a sort of friendly inter-
course Here we met several ladies we had 
previously known, in closer proximity than 
at the great receptions afterwards, as we
were fewer in number, and as the affair
was given in the “Members’ Mansions,”
which had been engaged for Council pur-
poses. On this occasion the Countess re- 
ceived alone, and stood on her feet for
about three hours. In looking back over
the entire time of this great gathering of
women from all parts of the world, we are
of the opinion that she was more thought-
ful and more considerate in providing for 
the well-being, comfort and entertainment
of members of the Council, than anybody 
could have imagined, or even desired. If
she does not have an attack of nervous
prostration after all is over, one would be
inclined to believe she had been given
superhuman strength.
	The next great reception was at Fulham 
Palace, the residence of the Lord Bishop of
London and his intellectual and accomplished
wife, Mrs. Creighton, who was one of the 
ablest women of the International Council,
and one who was instrumental in bringing

[Column 2]
about the success of the great undertaking
which brought the women of all civilized
lands together on one platform to discuss
the greatest and most momentous questions
of the day.
	Fulham Palace is very antique in ap-
pearance, and reminds one of the past cen-
turies; it is in the very atmosphere of the
house and grounds, though it is kept in ex-
cellent repair, and there have been, doubt-
less, many changes and improvements made
from time to time, yet one feels the sacred-
ness that pertains to ancient cathedrals,
and places where holy men have lived and
served and dispensed their learned lore, or
wisdom, such as is not vouchsafed to any
except those who devote their lives to the
great divine Master. It is impossible for
the writer to put upon paper in a few words
the emotions experienced while roaming
about under the shade of the trees centuries
old, and contemplating the scenes that must
have been enacted and events that had
transpired during the years that had flown
since the house and surroundings had been
occupied by Bishops and their curates and
rectors. At some future time we hope to
devote an article to our impressions of Ful-
ham Palace.
	On Monday, July 3, the Society of
American Women in London entertained
officers and members of the Council at a
luncheon and reception. The president,
Mrs. Griffin, Mrs. De Freise and Mrs. Fay
received the guests in elegant private par-
lors at Hotel Cecil, and the stars and stripes
were specially noticable in the draping and
decorating. Delicious refreshments were
handsomely served; but more of this here-
after.
	Tuesday afternoon, July 4, a garden
party was given by Lady Rothschild and
Mrs. Leopold de Rothschild, at Gunnersbury
Park, an elegant residence and park grounds
in the suburbs of London, where every-
thing was on the grandest scale imaginable;
indeed the papers said it was the most elab-
orate affair of its kind ever given in Lon-
don, certainly beyond any description the
writer will be able to give, such places
must be seen to be appreciated. At this re-
ception there were perhaps half of the Utah
ladies present. Lady Rothschild and her
daughter-in-law, Mrs. Leopold de Roths-
child, received and welcomed the guests on
their arrival, and every possible variety of
amusement had been provided in the
grounds, and refreshments were served in
the house during the entire afternoon. The
whole affair suggested wealth, luxuriance
of grandeur and beauty, and the greatest
hospitality and cordiality possible to ex-
press.
	The Countess of Warwick gave a garden
party and entertainment at her Hostel at
Reading, on the afternoon of July 5, which
was one of the most interesting occasions
we had the opportunity of participating in.
The guests spent the time in listening to
addresses in the garden, under the magnifi-
cent shade trees, by the Countess herself,
Lady Georgina Vernon and some titled
gentlemen, all upon practical subjects per-
taining to education and Reading College.
Tents were placed in near proximity where
refreshments were served, as also in the
house itself, which was very unique in all
its arrangements. After the exercises and
some social conversation, the grounds ad-
jacent were visited by the guests, and a few
of the ladies, accompanied by one of the
Professors from the College, visited the in-
stitution and went over almost the entire

[Column 3]
building. Reading College is built on the
site of an old abbey, and one part of it is
composed of the old abbey and cloisters.
	The Countess of Aberdeen, among other
social functions, gave a breakfast to all
officially connected with the Council, in
the private banqueting hall of the West-
minster Palace Hotel, and presented an ad-
dress of welcome to which one of the Ger-
man ladies responded in behalf of the
foreign delegates, and Dr. Stevenson, of
Chicago, for the United States, mentioning
especially that it was the 4th of July, In-
dependence day. Lord Aberdeen also had
a few words to say. Lady Marjorie Gor-
don assisted her mother at the breakfast,
which was a successful social function.
	The Countess also gave a Farewell, re-
ception at the Royal Institute of Water Col-
ors at the close of the session of the Coun-
cil. After issuing a thousand invitations,
some two or three hundred more were sent out,
it was a most eleborate entertainment. Lady
Marjorie assisted her mother in receiving,
and no one could doubt their desire to make
all happy, when they could stand an entire
evening and welcome each of the hundreds
of guests personally. The refreshments
were delicious, and served after the most
hospitable fashion. The water colors,
though of the finest one could imagine,
were lost sight of by many who preferred
living beauty in artistic costume. Really
the portraits on the walls, and the living
pictures that were moving about hither and
thither, flitting from room to room, were
brought into a sort of rivalry by the art
loving connoisseurs. The Countess spared
no pains or sacrifice to make every one hap-
py; she really gave herself for the time
being to the Council without limitation.
The women of the world are greatly in-
debted to the self-sacrifice of this philan-
thropic, heroice gentlewoman, who, with-
out display of ceremony, but with the true
instinct of royalty, devoted her valuable
time and magnificent talents to those who
came from afar, and like the Queen of Sheba
we can say, “the half had not been told.”
	The visit to Windsor Castle was entirely
due to her tact, influence and thoughtful-
ness. This visit has been described
by so many, that one scarcely feels to go
into detail; but Her Majesty, Queen Vic-
toria, cannot have too much said in her
praise as a woman, a mother and a sover-
eign of a great empire. It was most
gracious and lovely of the Queen of a great
realm on which the sun never sets, to favor
the delegation of the International Council
with Her Majesty’s presence in person, and
the ladies manifested their appreciation in
smiles and bows and waving their handker-
chiefs and singing in chorus, “God Save
the Queen.”
	The tea afterwards in St. George’s Hall,
where every appointment of the table was
significant of royalty, might well have been
called a banquet, so elaborate were the
preparations and so elegant the service.
	It was a fitting close to the great Inter-
national gathering of women of all lands,
that they should pay their respects to Her
Majesty, who in her life, public and pri-
vate, has been the embodiment of the high-
est and noblest qualities of true woman-
hood, a fitting example to the race. Dur-
ing the reign of this wonderful Queen much
has been done in forwarding the cause of
woman along various lines of learning, art
and science, and several special orders have
been introduced for the distinction of illus-
trious ladies. The profession of nursing


has been established on a permanent basis,
and great good has been the result of the
Queen’s sanction and assistance. Many
gateways have been opened whereby gentle
women (who have not fortunes at their
disposal) can obtain independent liveli-
hoods; altogether Her Majesty has proved
her desire to promote the best interests for
the happiest advance in the position of
women in the Victorian era.
	At a subsequent time we will mention
other receptions we had the privilege of
attending and some of the celebrated peo-
ple to whom we were introduced, also our
visits to Paris and Versailles, in France;
Liverpool, Birmingham, Manchester and
Nottingham, Warwick, Kenilworth, Leam-
ington and Cambridge, Stratford on Avon
and other notable places in England; and
Edinboro and Glasgow in Scotland.
	We have been asked many times both in
England and since our return to America,
what the outcome of this great Congress of
women from all lands will be, or what
great achievements will be the ultimate re-
sults? It is almost too soon to estimate
the gains that may accrue, but it is only
reasonable to suppose that a better under-
standing of the conditions of women in all
parts of the world, and, therefore, greater
success for any common or special cause.
That greater love and charity will prevail
among womankind cannot be doubted,
when such a fraternal feeling is fostered
and cherished as that which prevailed so
largely during the entire sessions of the
Council and Congress. Ultimately a great
federation of the sisterhood of the world
will come to pass by means of the processes
now at work, and that will mean for all
the world a higher type of civilization.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
LAYING OF CORNER STONE.
KEARN’S ST. ANN’S ORPHANAGE.
	The laying of the corner stone of the
Kearn’s St. Ann’s Orphanage, marks an
epcoh in the history of Utah of which all
loyal citizens may be justly proud; and it is
ardently desirable that other men and wom-
en, whom the Lord has blessed with
abundant means, may follow the noble ex-
ample thus given by Mr. and Mrs. Thomas
Kearns, who made it possible to establish
more permanently this charitable institu-
tion founded some time since by Bishop
Scanlan and the devoted Catholic ladies
and Sisters of Charity, by giving to St.
Ann’s Orphanage an edifice to which has
been appropriately added their name. The
building will be a very handsome one
when completed, and stands upon a large
plat of ground sufficient for its needs in the
way of play-grounds and other purposes.
	The ceremonies of laying the corner
stone were conducted by the Right Rev.
Bishop L. Scanlan, assisted by Vicar-Gen-
eral Dennis Kiely, under the waving stars
and stripes, and were of a very interesting
character. The exercises opened with
singing the hymn “Nearer my God to
Thee,” by the children’s choir, Miss Clara
Folek singing the solo. Then the corner
stone was laid by Bishop Scanlan, assisted
by Vicar General Kiely. The stone is
gray, three feet long and two wide. In
the box was placed a document containing
the following:
	The corner stone of the Kearn’s St. Ann’s
Orphanage, was laid on the 27th day of August,
1899, by the Right Rev. L. Scanlan; the supreme
pontiff at the time being Leo XIII.; the apostolic
delegate to the United States being Archbishop

[Column 2]
Sebastain Martinelli; the bishop of the diocese of
Salt Lake City being the Right Rev. L. Scanlan;
the President of the United States being William
McKinley; the Governor of the State of Utah
being Heber M. Wells, the Mayor of Salt Lake
City being John Clark; Utah’s representatives in
the Congress of the United States being Joseph L.
Rawlins, Senator, and Brigham H. Roberts, Con-
gressman.
	There has been deposited in this cavity: Copies
of the Salt Lake Tribune of August 27, 1899; the
Salt Lake Herald of August 27, 1899; the Deseret
Evening News of August 27, 1899, the Colorado
Catholic of August 26, 1899; a copy of the invita-
tion issued by the committee having in charge and
conducting the exercises of the laying of the corner
stone, and a copy of the program which was car-
ried out on this occasion. Coins of the United
States of the following values: One cent, a nickel,
a dime, twenty-five cents, a half dollar and a dol-
lar; a photograph of the Right Rev. L. Scanlan; a
photograph of Mr. and Mrs. Thomas Kearns and
children; a pamphlet, being a descriptive cata-
logue of the work of St. Mary’s academy, Salt
Lake City; a pamphlet, being a descriptive cata-
logue of the work of All Hallow’s college, Salt
Lake City.
	The contractors for the construction of the
orphanage are Marrion & Fielding.
	Salt Lake City, Utah, Aug. 27, 1899.
	The address of Bishop Scanlan was earn-
est, eloquent and tender, based upon the
familiar text found in the New Testament:
“Pure religion and undefiled before God
and the Father is this to visit the fatherless
and widows in their afflictions and to keep
himself unspotted from the world.” The
Bishop enlarged upon the subject of “What
means Christianity?” and especially applied
the scripture texts “What ye do unto the
least of these ye do unto me,” and “Love
thy neighbor as thyself;” also that man
was made in the image of God; he referred to
the writings of St. Augustine, in which he
said “No relationship is nearer than that
which exists between man and man.” The
whole discourse was exceedingly impressive
and might be studied as a lesson of divine
love and charity, it certainly could not fail
to produce good results. It is published in
full in the daily papers.
	After the address, Mrs. Sutton, of Park
City, sang with organ accompaniment,
“The Holy City,” and Governor H. M.
Wells delivered a brief and telling address,
in which he aptly included the text, “It is
more blessed to give than to receive.”
Hon. C. S. Varian then presented the
memorial trowel made from Silver King
bullion, to Mr. and Mrs. Kearns, the
founders of the edifice of which the corner
stone had just been laid. In Mr. Varian’s
speech he presented some facts in relation
to the struggles of the orphanage in the
past, and paid a high compliment to the
efforts of Bishop Scanlan and the Sisters of
Charity.
	Mr. Kearns responded with many affec-
tionate allusions to his wife and her desire
to endow such an institute, her devotion,
and that of the noble and self-sacrificing
Sisters of Charity, to motherless children,
and added on his own part, “Even in my
short life there was one bright moment, one
ray of sunshine when I wrote the check
that provided for them a home,” and al-
luding to the trowel, “In years to come
when mine eyes shall rest upon this little
treasure, it will remind me that shrouds
have no pockets, and true charity
knows no creeds, and while we enjoy the
luxuries of the world, that beneath this
roof, the Kearn’s St. Ann’s Orphanage,
must never sleep a hungry child.”
	Senator J. L. Rawlins made the closing
speech, which was a beautiful tribute to
heavenly charity, and the founders and
benefactors of the home. He expressed

[Column 3]
the wish, and thought that “others would
be led to cast their bread upon the waters,
and thus dedicate a portion of their wealth
to so good a cause.”
	The exercises concluded by singing the
national hymn, “America,” in which the
choir and audience joined.


RAISING MULBERRY TREES.
AN EXCELLENT REPORT FROM WAYNE CO.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:
	Thinking it might interest you and our
beloved president, Zina D. H. Young, to
know of our success in planting mulberry
trees and cuttings this last spring, and as
myself and counselors have recently visited
the southeast part of our Stake, I will tell
you of our beginning in the good work.
	Torrey Relief Society owns a city lot of
an acre and a quarter, and they have
planted seventy, three-year old trees from the
Salt Lake nursery, and about one thousand
yearling trees and cuttings.
	Caineville Relief Society has a city lot.
They planted out fifty, three-year old trees,
and four hundred yearlings and cuttings.
	Giles Relief Society also owns a lot, which
is not yet fenced. Brother White has planted
out on his own premises, one thousand cut-
tings and four hundred yearling trees for
the Relief Society, when the sisters get
their city lot fenced. He has twenty large
mulberry trees, ten years old, around his
home. I asked Sister White, who is presi-
dent of the Giles Relief Society, to make a
start next spring to feed silk worms. She said
she would give it a trial. We also distribu-
ted over one hundred yearling trees to
some friends in that part, which are all
growing nicely.
	Our efforts have been blessed in this
direction. Most of the trees planted have
grown and made good progress. We intend
to double our efforts next spring in plant-
ing out mulberry trees. We advised
some of the sisters to plant half an acre or
more on their own homesteads, and told
them that in time it would be a profitable
investment; besides it was an obligation
placed upon the sisters to make a success of
this enterprise. We have offered to furnish
young trees to those who cannot buy and
who will care for them, that we may get
this industry started.
	We had a very pleasant time holding
meetings, talking up this subject and get-
ting the brethren and sisters interested in
our labors. The Bishops attended our
meetings, and felt to extend a helping
hand. They all expressed their apprecia-
tion of the labors of the Relief Society, which
is encouraging, for we like to have the appro-
bation of our brethren.
			Yours respectfully,
				JANE S. COLEMAN.


AN aged lady getting into a cab in Dub-
lin, said to the driver, “Help me in, my
good man, for I am very old!” “Begor,
ma’am,” said he, “no matter what age you
are you don’t look it.”
	A VILLAGE schoolmaster entering school
one morning, read on the blackboard the
words: “Our teacher is a donkey!”
The pupils expected nothing less than, a
cyclone or an earthquake; but the philo-
sophic pedagogue contented himself with
adding the word “driver,” and opened the
school as usual.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE OLD FOLKS REMEMBERED.
	The first old folks’ day in Bingham Stake
was celebrated in an agreeable manner
at Brother Joseph Rich’s grove, he having
kindly offered the same for the occasion.
There not being many very old people here,
the age was put at forty-five, and every one
of that age, or over, was invited. Consider-
ing that it was harvest time, and a very
busy time, and near the Twenty-fourth
celebration, there was a pretty good atten-
dance.
	We were entertained with plenty of good
music from the Willow Creek band at the
commencement; then followed a hymn and
prayer, speeches, songs and recitations.
	Bishop Jardine in his remarks alluded to
the vast difference in our number now and
when he came here, the Saints at that time
being few and far between. When the
first ward was organized the first tithing
paid amounted to two dollars; now it
amounts to one thousand dollars.
	In the afternoon we had a short program,
after which they danced; and though I
know little about dancing, I very much en-
joyed seeing them, especially the dance
they call “Grand French Four.” Sister
Clark, I believe, received the prize for the
best step dancer.
	After the dance Brother Bybee inquired
who was the oldest brother on the platform,
and we found that Brother Williamson was.
He was awarded a cane, and the oldest sis-
ter, Abigail Campbell, received a rocking-
chair. Brother Bybee then asked the oldest
sister who had never had her ears pierced
to come forward. She was Sister Stokes,
and was promised a breast-pin. Then
Brother Bybee inquired who had given
birth to the most children. Sister M. L.
Bybee was the lady, having had fifteen chil-
dren. I believe her prize was a lamp.
	Every one went home looking happy.
We separated hoping to meet again next
year, and that none of the dear faces will be
missing.
				LITTLE SISTER.


[Column 1]
OBITUARY.
MARTHA V. STEINER.
	Died, June 6, 1899, at her residence in San
Diego, Califo[-]nia, of typhoid fever, Martha V.
Steiner, aged forty-one years.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas, an Alwise and Overruling Providence
has seen fit to call from this sphere of action, our
respected sister and friend, Martha V. Steiner;
and
	Whereas, she having proven herself an ener-
getic and faithful member of the M. I. A., and
a capable and efficient officer, having served as
secretary of said association, be it
	RESOLVED, That we truly appreciate her efforts
in the past, and the noble generosity of her nature,
which has often ministered to those in need; and,
be it further
	RESOLVED, That we truly mourn her loss and re-
gret her absence, and that we extend the hand of
sympathy to those whom her death has bereaved;
and, be it further
	RESOLVED, That a copy of these resolutions be
added to the M. I. A. record, a copy be furnished
the bereaved family, and one sent to the WOMAN’S
EXPONENT for publication.
						RHODA C. NASH.
	In behalf of the M. I. A. of San Diego, Califor-
nia.
IN THE MEMORY OF THE LATE SUSAN MAUDE VELLE
FAIRBANKS.
	Sister Fairbanks was born September 23, 1819,
in Bergen county, New Jersey, and died March 2,
1899. She became a member of the Church of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, February 2, 
1843 at Bergen, New Jersey, arrived in Salt

[Column 2]
Lake valley August 28, 1848, and moved to Pay-
son in the year 1851. She was the mother of thir-
teen children, ten of whom are how living,
seven sons and three daughters and a number of
grandchildren. Her husband died in 1895. She
was a faithful and energetic worker in the Relief
Society, being one of the early pioneers, having
passed through those trying scenes in the early
history of the Church, and also the hardships of
the settlement in this country. She bore her trials
with patience and fortitude and was always cheer-
ful. Her mind was bright and intelligent, she
was well informed on all leading topics, and she
was pleasant in conversation.
	Sister Fairbanks was a faithful Latter-day
Saint and has made a good record. She has gone
to her rest as a veteran in the church of God, and
as such will receive the reward of the faithful.
The funeral services were held in the Payson
tabernacle, Sunday, March 5, 1899. A large cor-
tege followed the remains to the cemetery.
			ANN PICKERING, Pres.
			JANE WORSENCROFT, Asst. Sec.
	First Ward Relief Society, Payson.
LAURA ALATHEA GARDNER.
	It becomes our painful duty to again record the
death of one of our faithful sisters, Sister Laura
Alathea Gardner, who died at Afton, Uintah
Co., Wyoming, (where she had gone for her health)
July 10, 1899. She was attended during her last
illness by her husband, one son, one daughter and
numerous relatives and friends.
	Her remains were brought to West Jordan, Salt
Lake Co., Utah, where the funeral was held July
14th. The body was taken to the Salt Lake City
cemetery for interment.
	The funeral was largely attended, and the
principal speakers were Presidents Angus M. Can-
non and Joseph E. Taylor, who dwelt upon the
many virtues and faithfulness of the deceased.
	Sister Laura Alathea Gardner was born August
3, 1834, at Alexandria, N. Y., and crossed the
plains to Utah in 1850. Married Archibald Gard-
ner March 3, 1852. She was the mother of eleven
children, eight sons and three daughters. Her
husband, four sons, two daughters and numerous
grandchildren survive her.
	She has always been a faithful member of the
Relief Society. When the branch in West Jordan
was organized in 1868, she was one of the first en-
rolled. In February, 1869, she was chosen presi-
dent, which office she held until the 12th of Decem-
ber, 1880, when she moved to Taylorsville.
	On July 6, 1891, Sister Gardner having moved
back to West Jordan, was chosen second counselor
to Sister Agnes Cutler, and subsequently the ward
was divided and Sister Agnes was in East Jordan.
The West Jordan Relief Society was reorganized
on January 13, 1896, and she was chosen first
counselor to President Marinda Bateman, which
office she held at the time of her death.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas, our Heavenly Father in His wisdom
has seen fit to call from our midst, on the 10th of 
July, A. D. 1899,  our beloved counselor, Laura
Alathea Gardner, we, the members of the West
Jordan Relief Society, do feel to mourn her loss;
and we know that we have lost a counselor and
kind friend.
	RESOLVED, That we do cherish her memory and
put into practice her wise teachings, and strive to
be kind, patient and charitable as she always was,
and try, as she did, to comfort the distressed and 
encourage the erring to lead a better life.
	RESOLVED, That we sympathize with her aged
husband and sorrowing family; and we pray that
our Heavenly Father, with His holy spirit, will
heal the wound thus caused by the loss of a loving
wife and mother.
	RESOLVED, That we send a copy of these resolu-
tions to the bereaved family, one to the WOMAN’S
EXPONENT, and that one be kept and inserted in
our minutes, as a record of our beloved counselor
and kind friend.
				MARINDA BATEMAN, Pres.
				DELILA GARDNER, Sec.
MARY CAROLINE HART.
	Sister Mary Caroline Hart, daughter of Brother
Levi R. and Sister Harriet M. Hart was born Oct.
17, 1884, at Salt Lake City, Utah, and lived at
Thomas Fork, Bear Lake Co., Idaho, where she
resided at the time of her death, July 22, 1899.
	She has always been a very tender and loving
child, and most exemplary young lady, and was
dearly loved by all who knew her. She was a
faithful member of the Sunday School, and for
the last three years has taken an active part in the 
Relief Society. She leaves a father, mother and

[Column 3]
five brothers to mourn her loss, her brother James
being in Germany filling a mission at the time of
her death.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas, our Father in Heaven, in His infinite
wisdom has seen fit to take from our midst one of
our esteemed sisters, Mary Caroline Hart, and 
	Whereas, in her demise we have lost a dear
friend and Latter-day Saint, therefore
	Resolved, That we, the officers and members of
the Thomas Fork Sunday School, do extend our
heartfelt sympathy to the bereaved family in this
their hour of sorrow.
	Resolved, That we ever cherish her memory
and regard her virtues as worthy of emulation.
	Resolved, that a copy of these resolutions be
presented to the bereaved family, one placed on
the records of the Sunday School and one sent to
the WOMAN’S EXPONENT for publication.
	THOMAS FORK SUNDAY SCHOOL.


A PLEASANT BIRTHDAY CELEBRATION
	A social gathering of friends, children
and grandchildren met at the residence of
Sister Lucinda Brown, in honor of her
seventy-fourth birthday.
	After partaking of a most delicious din-
ner, and thanking our Father in heaven for
the same, an impromptu program was
rendered. President Willis E. Robison
kindly consented to take charge of the
guests.
	After an appropriate selection from
the hymn book had been sung by all,
Patriarch E. H. Blackburn spoke very
commendably in behalf of our beloved sis-
ter. In his remarks he said that if he had
his hands upon the head of Sister Brown,
he felt he could say she was accepted of the
Lord.
	Presidents H. M. Hanson and G. S. Bas-
tian dwelt upon the excellent traits of
character Sister Brown must have pos-
sessed to train and raise her children in the
fear of God without a husband’s assistance.
She was always ready and willing to com-
fort those who were despondent and sad,
and was a friend in very deed to all.
	“O my Father, Thou that dwellest”
was played upon the mandolin and guitar
by Miss Ella Hunt and Mr. Bennie Robison.
	Counselors Nancy Blackburn and Eliza-
beth C. Jeffrey, of the Relief Society,
spoke words of encouragement, and praised
Sister Brown for her true integrity.
	Beautiful and appropriate music was
rendered by her children, Bishop Brown
and wife and Mrs. Sheffield.
	President Robison thought reunions of
this nature promoted a feeling of love to
draw us together and to elevate and broad-
en our views in our sphere of usefulness.
He was pleased to be associated with a wom-
an like Sister Brown, who had fearlessly
stood in defence of the principles of truth,
taught her family the Gospel in its purity
and gone through many trials to serve the
Lord; and during the past twenty-five
years he knew she had sought the best
society, and was proud to have her for a
friend. Felt that her actions would meet
the reward of again being associated with
her husband, who has been dead for many
years.
	Sister Robison thanked all for the
great honor shown her; felt it was more
than she was worthy of. Asked God to
bless all those present; that they might do
good on the earth.
	President Robison read the following
verses:
Dear husband, my soul shall treasure thy request
And when the night of fever and unrest
Melts in the morning of Eternity,
Like a freed bird then  I will come to thee.


I will come to thee in the morning sweet!
I have been true and soul with soul shall meet
Before God’s throne, and shall not be afraid;
Thou gav’st me trust, and it was not betrayed.

I will come to thee in the morning dear!
The night is dark, I do not know how near
The morning may be of that eternal day;
I can but keep my faithful watch, and pray.

I will come to thee in the morning love!
Wait for me on the eternal heights above;
The way is troubled where my feet must climb
Ere I shall reach the mountain top sublime.

I will come in the morning, O, my own!
But for a time must grope my way alone,
Through tears and sorrow, till the day shall dawn
And I shall hear the summons and pass on.

I will come in the morning, rest secure!
My hope is certain and my faith is sure;
After the gloom and darkness of the night
I will come to thee in the morning light.”

	After singing “When shall we all meet
again,” and prayer by Counselor Jacob
Bastian, all returned home, feeling the day
had been well spent.
				SARAH A. ROBISON.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


R. S. REPORTS.
MORGAN.
	The Relief Society conference of the
Morgan Stake convened in the Stake house,
August 3, 1899.
	Present on the stand were President
Lydia Rich and her counselors, President
Jane S. Richards, of Ogden, Sister Eliza-
beth Stevenson of Salt Lake City; also a
number of ward presidents. President
Richard Fry, of Morgan Stake, was also
present. 
	Morning session opened with singing 
and prayer, after which the statistical and
financial reports from five wards were read;
also the Stake report.
	Sisters Richards and Stevenson then
addressed the conference. They spoke on
the law of tithing, and bore strong testi-
mony to the truthfulness of it and of the
Gospel.
	Two p. m. After the usual exercises, 
five wards were reported: all were in
good condition.
	The speakers for the afternoon were
Counselors Webb and Tonks, and Sisters
Richards and Stevenson. The remarks of
the sisters were mostly on our home duties:
the training of children, keeping ourselves
pure, etc. They denounced the practice of
talking about our neighbors. 
	After singing, the meeting was dismissed
by Elder S. Francis.
				EMELINE WOOD, Sec.


U. W. P. C. 
	The regular monthly meeting of the U.
W. P. C. was held in the EXPONENT
office, Friday, June 30, 1899.
	The opening prayer was offered by Mrs. 
Ruth M. Fox, after which the secretary
called the roll, responses being given by 
quotations from Rudyard Kipling. 
	Mrs. A. M. Bradley, as representative of 
the Press Club, in behalf of the Federation
of Clubs, offered tickets to all for Field day 
at Saltair, Thursday, July 6th, the pro-
ceeds to be used for the free traveling
library.
	Mrs. Brown moved that the Press Club
spend a social day at Saltair, Thursday,
July 12th, which was seconded by Mrs. 
Fox: carried. Mrs. Aimee C. Schiller and 
Edith A Lovesy were appointed a commit-
tee of two to perfect all plans for the day.


A motion by Mrs. Bradley, seconded by
Mrs. Brown, that the club adjourn until
September, was carried.
	This being an evening with Kipling,
Mrs. I. Cameron Brown, the first on the
program, read a most interesting paper on
the life and poems of this well-known writ-
er. Mrs. Maria Francis followed with the
reading of one of Kipling’s masterpieces.
The latter was beautifully read, and proved
of great interest to all present. The pro-
gram closed with an interesting and care-
fully prepared paper on Current Events, by
Mrs. Rebecca H. Doolan.
	There being no further business, the
meeting adjourned.
			AIMEE C. SCHILLER, Sec.


NOTES.
	LADY SOMERSET is laboring to induce
London and other English cities to employ
matrons in police stations, after the fashion
of the United States.—Ex.
	No change of condition, be it ever so de-
sirable, can of itself change character, or
build up principle, or create virtue. And it
is upon these, after all, that we must de-
pend for real progress as well as for per-
manent happiness.
	ESTELLE REEL, U. S. Superintendent of
Indian Schools, in her annual report, says
that all the teachers among the Indians
agree that the solution of the problem of
Indian education lies in industrial training.
The studies that will enable the Indian lad
to earn an honest dollar by manual labor
are what he needs.—Ex.
	LADY GEORGINA VERNON, daughter of
the tenth Earl of Haddington, is well
known as an authority on dairying as an
occupation for women. She spent some
time in Normandy studying into French
methods of cheesemaking. She strongly
urges the practicability of dairying for
women as a self-supporting industry, but
says that cheese would be the most profit-
able branch of the work.—Ex.
	CLARA BARTON, president of the Red
Cross, has been visiting at the home of
Judge Joseph Sheldon in New Haven, Conn.
Miss Barton returned from Cuba about two
weeks ago. She was accompanied by her
general field-agent, Dr. Hubbell. The
rumors that have been in circulation for
weeks that Miss Barton would soon relin-
quish the management of the Red Cross
was denied by her friends. “She is in good
health notwithstanding her age,” said one
who has talked with her since her arrival
in this city. “Of course she is tired after
undergoing such hard work.”—Ex.
	THE House of Lords has done good work
in passing the second reading of the Shop
Assistants’ Bill, which provides for the
seating of women in shops. It has been
shown that appeals to humanity have failed
with the employers, and compulsory legis-
lation is therefore absolutely necessary to
stamp out this evil. Many young women
have been ruined for life by being compelled
to stand behind a counter for hours and
hours. Compulsory legislation has been
passed on the subject in some of the colonies
and several of the States of the American
Union; It is not reasonable that England
should lag behind. Where the health of
the people is concerned, all other consider-
ations must give way.—Ex.
